EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure tracked across South Dakota on the morning of February 18th, then moved to just north of the Twin Cities in Minnesota by the early evening. An area of snow over north central South Dakota in the early morning hours spread east-northeast, eventually setting up in an east-west band from New Rockford to Hillsboro to Bemidji. There were intense snow rates within this band through the remainder of the morning into the afternoon. Snow totals ranged from 6 to 15 inches along this corridor.

With shallow cold air in place, warm advection associated with a weak wave of low pressure moving northeast from Kansas to Wisconsin produced a shield of light precipitation across northwest Iowa. Most precipitation was in the form of snowfall, which totaled from 2 to 4 inches. Greatest accumulations were measured at Pipestone, Tracy and Marshall (4.0 inches).

Ejection of the main upper level wave (which had already steered a pair of snow producing systems across the area during the prior week) brought yet another light to moderate snowfall over northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska and southwest Minnesota. While it generally snowed for five to eight hours at most across southwest Minnesota, a majority of that time found visibility of a mile or less. Snowfall amounts 3 to 5 inches were common, and as a result of the snowfall rate, roads rapidly became snow-covered and hazardous.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of moderate snowfall pushed across southwest Minnesota, with four to six hours of moderate snowfall during the 1000CST to 1800CST window.  Representative snowfall amounts were 3.5 inches at Marshall, 4.0 inches at Worthington and Minneota, 4.8 inches at Tracy, 3.8 inches 2 miles northeast of Lakefield, 3.6 inches at Windom, and 3.0 inches at Pipestone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance rotating around a large Canadian upper vortex brought a period of enhanced frontogenetic forcing through the Missouri River valley and eastward across northwest Iowa and far southern Minnesota.  Snowfall averaged from 2 to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow over a period of several hours brought from 2 to 4 inches of snowfall across portions of southwest Minnesota near the Iowa Border.  Higher amounts were measured near Worthington (3.5 inches) and near Lakefield (1.8 inches).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air surged southward early on February 4th on gusty northerly winds of 20 to 35 mph.  Dangerous wind chills reached -25 to -35.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northerly winds from 20 to 35 mph brought dangerous wind chills colder than -20 for numerous hours, reaching as low as -34 at Windom, -32 at Jackson and -25 at Luverne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of Alberta clipper systems moving across the plains left behind a swath of light to moderate snow accumulations across southwest Minnesota. Much of the area received from 2 to 4 inches snowfall, while wind chills dropped below zero by the end of the time of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility dropped to less than a mile at times, producing hazardous travel conditions with snowfall totaling as much as 3.0 inches at Pipestone, 2.7 inches at Worthington, 2.2 inches 2 miles northeast of Lakefield, 2.1 inches at Windom and 2.0 inches at Lake Wilson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second disturbance in three days traversed the deep western trough, and the associated jet streak produced another area of widespread light to moderate snowfall across southwest Minnesota. Snowfall varied from 3 to 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of snow, occasionally moderate, resulted in an accumulation of 2.1 inches at Lake Wilson.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of snow, occasionally moderate, resulted in an accumulation of 4.0 inches at Windom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation began in the evening of the 10th and changed to all snow by morning. There were reports of over 6 inches of snow by daybreak. Most of the snow diminished greatly by 4 PM, but a gravity wave band of moderate to heavy snow continued into the early evening along the north shore and into eastern Carlton County. ||Snowfall amounts in the Minnesota Arrowhead ranged from 7 to over 9 inches. Specific amounts were 9.3 at Winton, 8.2 25 miles east of Ely, 8 at Soudan and 7.1 in Cook.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total measured 6.6 in Chisholm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of snow melt and rainfall brought minor flooding to portions the Rock River. A crest at minor flood stage occurred near Hardwick (HRDM5), impacting only lowland agricultural areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall along with snow melt caused minor flooding of lowland agricultural areas along upper portions of the Rock River. The river reached a crest of 2.2 feet above flood stage near Hardwick (gage site HRDM5) on March 6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of snow melt and a minor ice jam produced minor flooding on Pipestone Creek. A crest at minor flood stage occurred at Pipestone (PIPM5), impacting lowland agricultural areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water levels with snow melt and a minor ice jam resulted in minor flooding along Pipestone Creek. The river reached a crest of 0.3 feet above flood stage at Pipestone (gage site PIPM5) on March 20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense negative tilt upper-level trough pushed out across the Rockies and into the Plains on March 5, deepening into a very strong closed low across Iowa by early on March 6. 500 hPa heights fell as much as 27 dam in 24 hours as the system deepened. As a result, winds increased to a gusty 35 to 45 mph and produced brief periods of near blizzard conditions. The system was dynamic enough to produce a few thunderstorms early on March 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to briefly heavy snowfall totaled 7 to 12 inches, including 11.1 inches at Minneota and 7.5 inches at Marshall. East to northeast winds gusted from 30 to 40 mph, peaking at 41 mph at Marshall at 2116CST March 5, and created brief instances of near blizzard conditions between 1200CST to 1400CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense negative tilt upper-level trough pushed out across the Rockies and into the Plains on March 5, deepening into a very strong closed low across Iowa by early on March 6. 500 hPa heights fell as much as 27 dam in 24 hours as the system deepened. As a result, winds increased to a gusty 35 to 45 mph and produced brief periods of near blizzard conditions. The system was dynamic enough to produce a few thunderstorms early on March 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to briefly heavy snowfall totaled 6 to 11 inches, including 11.1 inches at Minneota and 7.5 inches at Marshall. East to northeast winds gusted from 30 to 40 mph, peaking at 41 mph at Marshall at 2116CST March 5, and created brief instances of near blizzard conditions between 1200CST to 1400CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of snow melt and rainfall brought minor flooding to portions of Split Rock Creek below Split Rock Lake.  Cresting at minor flood stage occurred below Jasper (JSPM5), impacting only lowland agricultural areas and a couple of low water crossings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall along with snow melt caused minor flooding of lowland agricultural areas along Split Rock Creek near Jasper. The river reached a crest of 1.9 feet above flood stage below Jasper (gage site JSPM5) on March 6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of snow melt and rainfall brought minor flooding to portions the Rock River. A crest at minor flood stage occurred near Hardwick (HRDM5), impacting only lowland agricultural areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall along with snow melt caused minor flooding of lowland agricultural areas along upper portions of the Rock River. The river reached a crest of 2.2 feet above flood stage near Hardwick (gage site HRDM5) on March 6.

Much of the Big Sioux basin received from one to two inches of liquid equivalent in the week leading up to the more significant flooding, including snow melt moisture from the historic blizzard on April 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and runoff from periods of heavier rainfall briefly produced minor flooding with significant impact to lowland agricultural areas and inundated sections of the Flandreau golf course. River levels reached 1.1 feet above flood stage on Flandreau Creek near Flandreau (gage site FCRS2) on April 23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough again progressed through the Rockies and evolved to a compact closed mid-level circulation drifting across eastern Nebraska and Iowa on April 18. Weak instability resulted in some isolated thunder near the Iowa border.  Snow increased rapidly during the pre-dawn hours, but also dissipated quickly in the late afternoon and evening, leaving behind between 2 and 6 inches snowfall across areas of southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed rapidly in the early morning hours after 0600CST.  Snowfall totaled 1.5 inches at Lake Wilson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northerly winds remained strong enough with the arctic push to produce several hours of dangerous wind chills during the evening of the 12th and early morning of the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northerly winds 5 to 12 mph along with temperatures from -5 to -15 produced wind chills below -20 overnight and early morning.  Minimum wind chills of -31 at Marshall, -26 at Slayton, -24 at Windom and -23 at Worthington and Jackson were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This event began on Friday, December 29, 2017. A frigid air mass dropped southward out of Canada, bringing some of the coldest air of the year. The morning of December 31, 2017 was the coldest, with many stations getting down to 25 below to 35 below zero. The coldest wind chill readings dipped to around 55 below zero.

With arctic air in place over southeast Minnesota, air temperatures dropped into the teens below zero during the morning of January 1st. When combined with wind speeds of 10 to 15 mph, wind chills of 35 to 40 below were common. These dangerously cold wind chills started in late December 2017.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 35 to 40 below were common across Dodge County during the morning of January 1st. The coldest reported wind chill by the automated weather observing equipment at the Dodge Center airport was 37 below.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration winter weather event ensued after Christmas as a full-latitude trough moved through the Rockies on December 26, initiating cyclogenesis as the mid-level wave closed off and lifted northeast from TX into KS by early on December 27. A leading pulse of warm advection ahead of the main system produced a swath of mixed precipitation on the 26th into early on the 27th across southeast SD and southwest MN. This area weakened before the main wave lifted across the area on the 27th and sheared into northern Minnesota by early morning December 28. A significant dry slot pushed across Iowa early on the 27th allowing for a brief change in precipitation to liquid or freezing precipitation. Heavier snowfall redeveloped within the TROWAL during the 27th and continued through the following night across southeast SD into southwest MN. ||As the area of low pressure strengthened moving across Iowa during the afternoon of December 27, winds increased with gusts of 40 to 55 mph common into the early morning of December 28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed early in the morning on December 26, changing briefly to light freezing rain between 1800-2300CST. Snow redeveloped during the overnight hours, becoming moderate to heavy through the afternoon of December 27 with visibility as low as one half mile or less at times. Northwest winds increased with gusts commonly from 30 to 40 mph from 1500CST December 27 through 0900CST December 28. Snowfall accumulated to 6.0 inches at Lake Wilson with a peak wind gust of 37 mph at Slayton. No travel was recommended during the height of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level trough pushing through the region failed to phase a northern and southern stream component, resulting in limited and fairly light snowfall, but a period of strong and gusty northwest winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Less than an inch of snowfall occurred during the overnight and morning hours, but with winds gusting to 40 to 50 mph, visibility fell to a mile or less briefly creating travel difficulty. Strong wind gusts continued well through the daylight hours on January 11 and maintained some areas of blowing snow with the base of an inch or two snow cover in place prior to the new snowfall. Peak winds were measured at 50 mph at Jackson, 49 mph near Rushmore, 46 mph at Pipestone, 44 mph at Windom and 43 mph at Marshall.

A major winter storm developed across the central Rockies Sunday January 21st, and moved northeast across the Plains, and into the Upper Midwest by early Tuesday morning, January 23rd. A swath of heavy snow fell from south central, into east central Minnesota, as well as portions of west central Wisconsin Monday, January 22nd. The heaviest snow fell along a line from south central Minnesota, near St. James, northeast to the southern part of the Twin Cities Metro area, and into west central Wisconsin near Hudson, Rice Lake and Ladysmith. Snowfall amounts over 8 inches occurred in this band, with localized one foot totals near St. James, Jordan, Prior Lake, and St. Paul, Minnesota. Another heavier band of snow with amounts of over a foot occurred from Waseca, Owatonna, Faribault and Cannon Falls, Minnesota. There was also a very sharp cutoff on where the snow fell, and reports several inches, to only a trace in a 25 mile band on the northwest side of the storm system. In addition to the snowfall, strong winds developed in southern Minnesota, with gusts of 35 to 45 mph at times from New Ulm, to St. Peter and Owatonna, south to the Iowa border. The Minnesota Department of Transportation indicated by 9 am that numerous roads were nearly impassable, and travel was not recommended (MNDOT Graphical Map) across the Blizzard warned area. ||Some of the higher totals include;||17 inches at Winnebago, |16.6 inches in Prior Lake, |16 inches in Fairmont, |16 inches in Owatonna, |16 inches in Nerstrand. ||Travel was significantly impacted with travel times more than tripled during the afternoon rush hour. From the MN State Patrol, statewide from midnight to 8:45 pm, Monday the 22nd of January, there were 231 crashes, 14 with injuries, 1 serious, but 0 fatal. 560 flights into and out of MSP airport had been cancelled and another 114 delayed. At one point, the airport closed due to snowfall rates more than 1.5 inches per hour. A spokeperson at the airport indicated that during the day there were at least two occasions where all four runways had to be shut down for several hours.  |Portions of I-35 was closed for a time between Owatonna and Faribault.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports from the public, trained weather observers, and local airports indicated that a mixture of light rain, sleet and snow, changed over to all snow by late Monday. Total snowfall amounts by the time it ended Monday evening, ranged from 8 to 10 inches across the northern part of the county, to around 4 inches in the far south.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm passed through southeast Minnesota on January 22nd and dropped over a foot of snow at some locations. The heaviest snow fell from Austin (Mower County) to Minnesota City (Winona County) and to the north. The highest reported snow totals were 12 inches in Byron (Olmsted County), 13.5 inches in Kasson (Dodge County) and 14 inches in West Concord (Dodge County). During the period of heaviest snow, the visibility was often reduced to a half mile or less and northeast winds gusting to 35 to 40 mph created some blowing and drifting snow. Most schools closed ahead of the storm for January 22 and some were closed again on the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 5 to 6 inches of snow across Mower County. The highest reported total was 6.4 inches in Austin. Wind gusts to 30 mph created some blowing and drifting snow.

A major winter storm developed across the central Rockies Sunday January 21st, and moved northeast across the Plains, and into the Upper Midwest by early Tuesday morning, January 23rd. A swath of heavy snow fell from south central, into east central Minnesota, as well as portions of west central Wisconsin Monday, January 22nd. The heaviest snow fell along a line from south central Minnesota, near St. James, northeast to the southern part of the Twin Cities Metro area, and into west central Wisconsin near Hudson, Rice Lake and Ladysmith. Snowfall amounts over 8 inches occurred in this band, with localized one foot totals near St. James, Jordan, Prior Lake, and St. Paul, Minnesota. Another heavier band of snow with amounts of over a foot occurred from Waseca, Owatonna, Faribault and Cannon Falls, Minnesota. There was also a very sharp cutoff on where the snow fell, and reports several inches, to only a trace in a 25 mile band on the northwest side of the storm system. In addition to the snowfall, strong winds developed in southern Minnesota, with gusts of 35 to 45 mph at times from New Ulm, to St. Peter and Owatonna, south to the Iowa border. The Minnesota Department of Transportation indicated by 9 am that numerous roads were nearly impassable, and travel was not recommended (MNDOT Graphical Map) across the Blizzard warned area. ||Some of the higher totals include;||17 inches at Winnebago, |16.6 inches in Prior Lake, |16 inches in Fairmont, |16 inches in Owatonna, |16 inches in Nerstrand. ||Travel was significantly impacted with travel times more than tripled during the afternoon rush hour. From the MN State Patrol, statewide from midnight to 8:45 pm, Monday the 22nd of January, there were 231 crashes, 14 with injuries, 1 serious, but 0 fatal. 560 flights into and out of MSP airport had been cancelled and another 114 delayed. At one point, the airport closed due to snowfall rates more than 1.5 inches per hour. A spokeperson at the airport indicated that during the day there were at least two occasions where all four runways had to be shut down for several hours.  |Portions of I-35 was closed for a time between Owatonna and Faribault.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports from the public, trained weather observers, and local airports indicated that light snow began Monday morning. Wind speeds increased to above 35 mph, with gusts up to 40 mph at times during the afternoon. This was accompanied by heavy snow at times. Although the the blizzard conditions abated by 5 pm, light snow continued to fall through late evening. Total snowfall amounts by the time it ended Monday evening, ranged from 10 to 14 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level trough pushing through the region failed to phase a northern and southern stream component, resulting in limited and fairly light snowfall, but a period of strong and gusty northwest winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Less than an inch of snowfall occurred during the overnight and morning hours, but with winds gusting to 40 to 50 mph, visibility fell to a mile or less briefly creating travel difficulty. Strong wind gusts continued well through the daylight hours on January 11 and maintained some areas of blowing snow with the base of an inch or two snow cover in place prior to the new snowfall. Peak winds were measured at 50 mph at Jackson, 49 mph near Rushmore, 46 mph at Pipestone, 44 mph at Windom and 43 mph at Marshall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The morning of Saturday, February 24th, a storm system moved out of the Plains, and into the Upper Midwest.  ||Several waves of snow, mixed with sleet and freezing drizzle at times in south central, and east central Minnesota, moved across Minnesota, and into western Wisconsin during the day Saturday. The first wave of light snow moved across southwest, west central, and central Minnesota during the morning, but most areas only received a light dusting with some sleet noted along the Iowa border. By the afternoon, the snow had spread across the rest of southern Minnesota, and into western Wisconsin. ||During the mid to late afternoon, the snowfall became heavier in Minnesota, and pivoted to the east-northeast before tapering off during the evening in eastern Minnesota. ||Snowfall amounts varied across central, and southern Minnesota, with the heavier bands from near New Ulm in south central Minnesota, northeast to the western suburbs of the Twin Cities, and into west central Wisconsin around Osceola, and St. Croix Falls. Farther to the south and southeast across Minnesota, especially along the Iowa border, the mixture of sleet and freezing rain during the day, kept snowfall amounts much lower than expected. Most areas that had a mixture of sleet and freezing rain received between 3 and 5 inches of snow. Elsewhere, more snow led to higher totals. ||Another area of heavier snowfall occurred from Alexandria, eastward into Todd and Morrison Counties of central Minnesota during the afternoon, and early evening. ||Some of the heavier totals included:||9.0 Inches near Princeton and Orrock in Sherburne County. |9.0 Inches near St. Francis in Anoka County. |9.0 Inches near Alexandria in Douglas County. |8.0 Inches near New Germany & Norwood/Young America in Carver County.|8.0 Inches near Cambridge in Isanti County. |8.0 Inches near Hutchinson in McLeod County.|8.0 Inches near Gaylord in Sibley County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of information measured between 6 and 9 inches of snow across the county between the mid afternoon through late evening. The heaviest occurred in the early evening with snowfall rates of 1 inch per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This event began on Friday, December 29, 2017. A frigid air mass dropped southward out of Canada, bringing some of the coldest air of the year. The morning of December 31, 2017 was the coldest, with many stations getting down to 25 below to 35 below zero. The coldest wind chill readings dipped to around 55 below zero.

A major winter storm developed across the central Rockies Sunday January 21st, and moved northeast across the Plains, and into the Upper Midwest by early Tuesday morning, January 23rd. A swath of heavy snow fell from south central, into east central Minnesota, as well as portions of west central Wisconsin Monday, January 22nd. The heaviest snow fell along a line from south central Minnesota, near St. James, northeast to the southern part of the Twin Cities Metro area, and into west central Wisconsin near Hudson, Rice Lake and Ladysmith. Snowfall amounts over 8 inches occurred in this band, with localized one foot totals near St. James, Jordan, Prior Lake, and St. Paul, Minnesota. Another heavier band of snow with amounts of over a foot occurred from Waseca, Owatonna, Faribault and Cannon Falls, Minnesota. There was also a very sharp cutoff on where the snow fell, and reports several inches, to only a trace in a 25 mile band on the northwest side of the storm system. In addition to the snowfall, strong winds developed in southern Minnesota, with gusts of 35 to 45 mph at times from New Ulm, to St. Peter and Owatonna, south to the Iowa border. The Minnesota Department of Transportation indicated by 9 am that numerous roads were nearly impassable, and travel was not recommended (MNDOT Graphical Map) across the Blizzard warned area. ||Some of the higher totals include;||17 inches at Winnebago, |16.6 inches in Prior Lake, |16 inches in Fairmont, |16 inches in Owatonna, |16 inches in Nerstrand. ||Travel was significantly impacted with travel times more than tripled during the afternoon rush hour. From the MN State Patrol, statewide from midnight to 8:45 pm, Monday the 22nd of January, there were 231 crashes, 14 with injuries, 1 serious, but 0 fatal. 560 flights into and out of MSP airport had been cancelled and another 114 delayed. At one point, the airport closed due to snowfall rates more than 1.5 inches per hour. A spokeperson at the airport indicated that during the day there were at least two occasions where all four runways had to be shut down for several hours.  |Portions of I-35 was closed for a time between Owatonna and Faribault.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports from the public, trained weather observers, and local airports indicated that light snow began early Monday morning. Heavy snow fell in the morning, and through the afternoon before tapering off around midnight. Total snowfall amounts by the time it ended, ranged from 12 to 16 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm developed across the central Rockies Sunday January 21st, and moved northeast across the Plains, and into the Upper Midwest by early Tuesday morning, January 23rd. A quick moving clipper system deepened rapidly and carved out a closed upper level low across the western Great Lakes as it dropped southeast from the Canadian prairie provinces on the evening of January 14. The system produced from one to three inches of snow through January 15, as well as a brutal rush of arctic air into the region with winds gusting up to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusting to 35 to 45 mph along with temperatures falling to subzero levels produced dangerous to life threatening wind chills from -35 to -45. Wind chill readings bottomed out at -41 at Windom, -38 at Marshall and -37 at Slayton, Worthington and Jackson. Wind chills colder than -20 persisted consistently through 1700CST January 16. One to three inches snow fell, including 2.8 inches at Minneota, 2.7 inches at Tracy and 2.6 inches 2 miles northeast of Lakefield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving clipper system deepened rapidly and carved out a closed upper level low across the western Great Lakes as it dropped southeast from the Canadian prairie provinces on the evening of January 14. The system produced from one to three inches of snow through January 15, as well as a brutal rush of arctic air into the region with winds gusting up to 45 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusting to 35 to 45 mph along with temperatures falling to subzero levels produced dangerous to life threatening wind chills from -35 to -45. Wind chill readings bottomed out at -41 at Windom, -38 at Marshall and -37 at Slayton, Worthington and Jackson. Wind chills colder than -20 persisted consistently through 1700CST January 16. One to three inches snow fell, including 2.8 inches at Minneota, 2.7 inches at Tracy and 2.6 inches 2 miles northeast of Lakefield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure area developed across Kansas and lifted into Iowa by December 2. The leading edge of the system produced some light ice accumulations from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa on early Saturday morning. Heavy snow and wind developed during Saturday and Saturday evening, but snowfall was diminished quite a bit by downslope impacts west of the Buffalo Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of freezing rain which produced a glaze of light icing during the early morning of December 1, heavy snow occurred for numerous hours. Accumulations over less than a 24-hour period totaled 8.0 inches four miles north of Currie. Treacherous travel resulted as winds gusted to 30 to 40 mph and combined with snowfall to produce visibility of a half mile or less at times between 1300-1800CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration winter weather event ensued after Christmas as a full-latitude trough moved through the Rockies on December 26, initiating cyclogenesis as the mid-level wave closed off and lifted northeast from TX into KS by early on December 27. A leading pulse of warm advection ahead of the main system produced a swath of mixed precipitation on the 26th into early on the 27th across southeast SD and southwest MN. This area weakened before the main wave lifted across the area on the 27th and sheared into northern Minnesota by early morning December 28. A significant dry slot pushed across Iowa early on the 27th allowing for a brief change in precipitation to liquid or freezing precipitation. Heavier snowfall redeveloped within the TROWAL during the 27th and continued through the following night across southeast SD into southwest MN. ||As the area of low pressure strengthened moving across Iowa during the afternoon of December 27, winds increased with gusts of 40 to 55 mph common into the early morning of December 28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed early in the morning on December 26, changing briefly to light freezing rain between 1800-2300CST. Snow redeveloped during the overnight hours, becoming moderate to heavy through the afternoon of December 27 with visibility as low as one half mile or less at times. Northwest winds increased with gusts commonly from 30 to 40 mph from 1200CST December 27 through 0600CST December 28. Snowfall accumulated to 7.9 inches at Pipestone with a peak wind gust of 36 mph. No travel was recommended during the height of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level trough pushed across the Rockies on January 21, and developed into a closed cyclone across the central Plains. As low pressure deepened moving from Kansas to the Great Lakes on January 22, precipitation wrapped back into a tight deformation zone on the northwest flank of the storm. Snow developed late on January 21, and was followed by a rapid increase of northwest winds to a gusty 30 to 40 mph early on January 22. Visibility at times dipped to around a mile with falling and/or blowing snow, creating difficult travel conditions. On the northern gradient of the main snow band, snowfall totals varied from considerably from less than an inch to around 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed late on January 21 and continued through mid afternoon January 22. Winds increased overnight into the morning hours of the 22nd, gusting from 30 to 40 mph at times and creating areas of blowing snow with visibility a mile or less at times.  Peak winds on January 22 reached 39 mph at Pipestone around 0735CST, 36 mph at Tracy at 1055CST, and 32 mph at Marshall around 0951CST.  Snowfall totalled 4.2 inches at Tracy, 0.5 inches at Marshall, and 1.1 inches at Pipestone.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a period of light wintry mix, snow developed and winds increased across far southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, far northwest Iowa and northeast Nebraska. Although wind speeds dropped some from the previous day, wind speeds averaged around 10 mph, which kept wind chill values near -35F. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources reported that wind chill values averaged around 35 degrees below zero early Monday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of Arctic air continued to move southward across North America during the first few weeks of January. This Arctic air intrusion dropped temperatures between 10 and 15 degrees below zero in west central Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of Monday, January 15th. As gusty winds continued in this region due to the wake of strong high pressure building across the area, wind chill values dropped to -35F for several hours before wind speeds diminished Tuesday morning, January 16th. ||The coldest wind chills included;||Starbuck in Pope County at -40F.|Morris in Swift County at -40F.|Clara City in Chippewa County at -37F.|Benson in Stevens County at -36F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observations across the county reported temperatures in the -10 to -15F range, and gusty winds of 10 to 15 mph. This created wind chill values around -35F, for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota on Wednesday January 10th. Temperatures were quite mild to start the day, in the mid 20s to lower 30s. Dew point values were also high, or in the mid 20s to around 30, which resulted in some areas of fog and freezing precipitation. Temperatures fell quickly behind the cold front by late morning into the afternoon, as snow also moved into the area. North winds picked up by the afternoon and evening of the 10th, resulting in blizzard conditions over the central and northern Red River Valley. The strongest winds actually held off until Thursday the 11th, when the Grand Forks airport reported a wind gust to 54 mph and the Fargo airport reported 52 mph. The most snow fell between the highway 2 and 200 corridors, where about 6 to 7 inches of snow was reported. Many schools and businesses were closed on the 11th. The blizzard shut down U. S. Highway 2 from East Grand Forks to Crookston.

A strong mid-level trough pushed across the Rockies on January 21, and developed into a closed cyclone across the central Plains. As low pressure deepened moving from Kansas to the Great Lakes on January 22, precipitation wrapped back into a tight deformation zone on the northwest flank of the storm. Northwest winds rapidly increased to a gusty 35 to 50 mph after snow developed early on January 22. Widespread visibility of a quarter mile or less with snow, heavy at times, continued through late afternoon. Snowfall amassed to 8 to 13 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed around 0500CST January 22, with northwest winds increasing during the early morning to a gusty 40 to 50 mph. Frequent wind gusts along with heavy falling snow produced visibility frequently near whiteout starting early morning, which continued through late afternoon. A peak wind of 45 mph was measured at Worthington at 1455CST. Schools were closed across the area, and the Minnesota Department of Transportation recommended no travel in southwest Minnesota at 1100CST due to deteriorated conditions. Weather conditions gradually improved toward 1700CST, but travel was not advised well into the night. Total snowfall of 11.1 inches was measured at Worthington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level trough pushed across the Rockies on January 21, and developed into a closed cyclone across the central Plains. As low pressure deepened moving from Kansas to the Great Lakes on January 22, precipitation wrapped back into a tight deformation zone on the northwest flank of the storm. Snow developed as northwest winds rapidly increased to a gusty 35 to 45 mph early on January 22. Widespread visibility of a mile or less at time occurred with falling snow, heavy at times, which continued through late afternoon. Snowfall totaled to 3 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm with snow and widespread blowing snow developed early on January 22. Minnesota Department of Transportation officials recommended no travel across southwest Minnesota starting around 0900CST, with visibility frequently at a mile or less, and briefly down to around a quarter mile.  Winds gusted as high as 40 mph at Slayton around 1100CST and 37 mph at Luverne around 0900CST.  Total snowfall tallied 4 to 7 inches, with 5.0 inches measured at Lake Wilson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air rushed southward into the region behind light snow producing system on the evening of January 11 and through the morning of January 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northerly winds at 10 to 20 mph combined with near zero and subzero temperatures to produce wind chills from 20 below to 30 below zero. Minimum wind chill readings of -34 at Slayton, Windom and Worthington, -32 at Jackson, -31 degrees at Marshall and -30 at Pipestone were recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick winter storm moved across the Northern Plains, and into the Upper Midwest and western Great Lakes the night of Friday March 30th, through Saturday, March 31st. ||Snow began to develop across western Minnesota during the evening of March 30th, and quickly intensified from west of the Twin Cities as it lifted east, and northeast. ||Several rounds of 1 inch per hour rates were noted near the Twin Cities, and northeast into west central Wisconsin toward midnight. A solid area of light to moderate snow began to develop in west central, and central Minnesota as the next stronger wave moved across the Northern Plains. ||Several hours of snowfall rates of one half to one inch per hour occurred from Alexandria, east to St. Cloud and Rush City, Minnesota. ||Some of the higher totals from the storm include:||Isle, Minnesota in Mille Lacs County had 11 inches. |Camp Ripley in Morrison County had 10.6 inches. |Little Fallls in Morrison County had 10 inches.|Rush City in Chisago County had 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snowfall amounts across Isanti County ranged from 6 to 9 inches by sunrise Saturday morning, March 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-hitting storm with heavy snow began overnight of the 30th and ended from the west in the morning. The heaviest snow fell over southern areas of the forecast area, from southern Cass County east to Pine County and southern Carlton County. Snow amounts ranged from 6 to nearly 13 inches. Snow amounts include 11.4 near Malmo, 10.5 near Casino, Brainerd with 9, and Sandstone with 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ejection of the main upper level wave (which had already steered a pair of snow producing systems across the area during the prior week) brought yet another light to moderate snowfall over northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska and southwest Minnesota. While it generally snowed for five to eight hours at most across southwest Minnesota, a majority of that time found visibility of a mile or less. Snowfall amounts 3 to 5 inches were common, and as a result of the snowfall rate, roads rapidly became snow-covered and hazardous.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of moderate snowfall pushed across southwest Minnesota, with four to six hours of moderate snowfall during the 1000CST to 1800CST window.  Representative snowfall amounts were 3.5 inches at Marshall, 4.0 inches at Worthington and Minneota, 4.8 inches at Tracy, 3.8 inches 2 miles northeast of Lakefield, 3.6 inches at Windom, and 3.0 inches at Pipestone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With shallow cold air in place, warm advection associated with a weak wave of low pressure moving northeast from Kansas to Wisconsin produced a shield of light precipitation across southwest Minnesota. While most precipitation was a mix of light freezing rain, freezing drizzle and light snow on February 19 which resulted in a 0.05 to 0.15 inch ice accumulation, a second round of precipitation early on February 20 brought a dusting of less than an inch of snow which coated the ice and produced hazardous travel conditions. For the two day period, school delays were common for those not already out for the Washington's Birthday holiday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second disturbance in three days traversed the deep western trough, and the associated jet streak produced another area of snowfall across southwest Minnesota. A narrow band of heavier snowfall produced accumulations from 4 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall, briefly moderate to heavy from 1700CST through 2200CST, totaled up to 7 inches in less than 12 hours.  Accumulations were measured at 7 inches near Pipestone.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minnesota Department of Transportation site MN061 2 miles west of Schley measured wind chill of minus 40 at 8:20 AM.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill measured minus 41 reported at Duluth International Airport 8:55 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of freezing drizzle and freezing rain on February 19th and 20th produced a coating of ice on untreated surfaces across southeast Minnesota. The precipitation fell with temperatures below freezing as a slow moving area of low pressure moved from the Missouri River Valley toward the Great Lakes. Ice accumulations up to a quarter of an inch were reported on Mower County roads and most schools were either closed or delayed on the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Untreated surfaces became coated in ice as a prolonged period of freezing drizzle and rain occurred across Mower County. Law enforcement reported up to a quarter inch of accumulation on roads across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense surface low pressure area brought scattered showers and thunderstorms along with heavy snow to west central Minnesota from the 5th to the 6th.  The scattered showers and thunderstorms moved across the region during the early morning hours of the 5th while snow developed by mid morning.  There were some reports of thundersnow. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 11 inches before it ended on the 6th. The heavy snow resulted in closed businesses and schools along with difficult, if not, impossible travel.  Many activities and events were also postponed or cancelled.

A male who was not wearing a seat belt  was transported to an area hospital where he succumbed to his injuries a few days later. Another crash occurred later in the afternoon around 4:15 PM when a vehicle lost control and entered the median and rolled multiple times. A passenger in the vehicle was killed while another sustained life-threatening injuries. The driver of this vehicle did not report any injuries.||A winter weather advisory was in effect during the crashes for a few inches of snowfall and slick roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely slow moving storm system with multiple periods of snow affected portions of east-central Minnesota. The first batch of snow that fell over the region began on the afternoon of the 4th. By sunrise the Brainerd area had 7 inches with more falling through the late morning. Another band of snow developed from Carlton and Pine counties and continued into the evening.  Winds were also very strong on the 5th with gusts well into the 40 mph range. The winds caused sporadic power outages. Specific snow amounts include 13.5 5 miles east of Leader, 13 a few miles south of Brainerd at Shepard, 12.7 near Casino, 8.5 9.1 at East Gull Lake, 6 at Walker, 9.3 near Bruno and 10 near Moose Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow began across the Northland during the late afternoon and early evening. By midnight some areas had already received close to 6 inches of snow, with more falling  overnight. The western end of Lake Superior and the north shore, including the Arrowhead region, received the most snow. Finland had 14 inches of snow, with Duluth at nearly 8 inches and Island Lake at 8.5 inches. Portions along Interstate-35 received up to 8 inches of snow. Some areas received less than 6 inches of snow but the snowfall rate and strong winds made travel conditions difficult. The average wind speed at Duluth was 26 mph with a peak wind of 36 mph, all from the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer in Kabetogama measured a 24-hour snowfall total of 8.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely slow moving storm system with multiple periods of snow affected portions of east-central Minnesota. The first batch of snow that fell over the region began on the afternoon of the 4th. By sunrise the Brainerd area had 7 inches with more falling through the late morning. Another band of snow developed from Carlton and Pine counties and continued into the evening.  Winds were also very strong on the 5th with gusts well into the 40 mph range. The winds caused sporadic power outages. Specific snow amounts include 13.5 5 miles east of Leader, 13 a few miles south of Brainerd at Shepard, 12.7 near Casino, 8.5 9.1 at East Gull Lake, 6 at Walker, 9.3 near Bruno and 10 near Moose Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of snow developed over northeast Minnesota. The main snow area was along and north of U.S. Highway 2 in Minnesota where amounts ranged from 6 to 15 inches. The highest amounts were from northern Cass County across much of Itasca, central St. Louis County and all of the Arrowhead region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow of 8.0 measured 12 miles north of Grand Rapids.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow fell across the entire forecast area. Snow began in the evening of the 22nd and ended around sunrise on the 23rd. Highest snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to about 11 inches from Crow Wing County, through northern Aitkin County to central and southern St. County.  Much of Crow Wing County had 7.5 to 8 inches of snow in a less than 12 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station 2 miles west of Leonidas reported a snowfall measurement of 6.0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow began across the Northland during the late afternoon and early evening. By midnight some areas had already received close to 6 inches of snow, with more falling  overnight. The western end of Lake Superior and the north shore, including the Arrowhead region, received the most snow. Finland had 14 inches of snow, with Duluth at nearly 8 inches and Island Lake at 8.5 inches. Portions along Interstate-35 received up to 8 inches of snow. Some areas received less than 6 inches of snow but the snowfall rate and strong winds made travel conditions difficult. The average wind speed at Duluth was 26 mph with a peak wind of 36 mph, all from the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported of 8.3 inches of snowfall 4 miles south-southwest of Snow Bank Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With shallow cold air in place, warm advection associated with a weak wave of low pressure moving northeast from Kansas to Wisconsin produced a shield of light precipitation across southwest Minnesota. While most precipitation was a mix of light freezing rain, freezing drizzle and light snow on February 19 which resulted in a 0.05 to 0.15 inch ice accumulation, a second round of precipitation early on February 20 brought a dusting of less than an inch of snow which coated the ice and produced hazardous travel conditions. For the two day period, school delays were common for those not already out for the Washington's Birthday holiday.

On the 5th, the -40 to -45 wind chills were widespread north of U.S. Highway 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chishom-Hibbing Airport measured a -43 wind chill. COOP observer in Embarrass had an observation temperature of 42 below at 6 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of freezing drizzle and freezing rain on February 19th and 20th produced a coating of ice on untreated surfaces across southeast Minnesota. The precipitation fell with temperatures below freezing as a slow moving area of low pressure moved from the Missouri River Valley toward the Great Lakes. Ice accumulations up to a quarter of an inch were reported on Mower County roads and most schools were either closed or delayed on the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Untreated surfaces became coated in ice as a prolonged period of freezing drizzle and rain occurred across Olmsted County. Ice accumulations of an eighth of an inch were reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of the 25th, the Cass County Sheriff's Office received a report of an unresponsive male in a fish house on Leech Lake. The 21 year old male was pronounced deceased upon arrival at the hospital. The medical examiner ruled cause of death as accidental carbon monoxide poisoning. Overnight low temperatures were in the upper teens.

Another amplifying wave in rapid west-northwest flow aloft brought a quick shot of snowfall to the region. With lapse rates slightly unstable on the warm side of the baroclinic zone, strong frontogenetic forcing resulted in a narrow band and brief of very intense snowfall which resulting in less than a half mile visibility at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall developed quickly in the morning and remained heavy at times into early afternoon.  Southeast wind gusted to 30-35 mph and resulted in periods of visibility from one-quarter to one-half mile at times.  Snowfall reached 6.5 inches 4 miles north of Currie and 6.1 inches at Minneota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeast Minnesota was covered with 4 to 8 inches of snow on March 5th. In addition to the snow, wind gusts of 35 to 60 mph created near white-out conditions at times with lots of blowing and drifting snow. Most schools in the area dismissed early on the 5th and then had a late start on the 6th. The highest reported snow amounts were 8.1 inches near Predmore (Olmsted County) and 8.2 inches near Theilmann (Wabasha County). The highest recorded wind gust was 60 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer observers reported that 5 to 7 inches of snow fell across Dodge County. The highest reported total was 7.8 inches near Kasson. Wind gusts of 40 to 45 mph created near white-out conditions at times from blowing and drifting snow. The highest recorded wind gust was 48 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Dodge Center airport and mesonet equipment near Kasson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most intense storm of the month, in fact many months, wrapped up across the central Plains on April 13, spreading a mix of wintry precipitation across the area, accompanied by numerous thunderstorms. Not included in this episode was the spotty severe hail and wind which occurred in southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota prior to the onset of snowfall during the morning and afternoon of April 13. |Eventually, influx of colder air would change all precipitation over to snow, and ushered in a record-breaking snowfall of 8 to 16 inches (some locations over 20 inches) and fierce winds gusting 50 to 60 mph to southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, northwest Iowa and extreme northeast Nebraska. Snow drifts over 6 feet were reported in many locations. Extremely low visibility persisted for almost a day for most locations, making travel very difficult if not impossible. ||One person was killed in Rock County when the snowmobile he was riding collided with an interstate guard rail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Life threatening conditions developed during the morning of April 14, as the earlier mix of rain, sleet and snow changed to all snow, and brutal winds (gusting as high as 55 mph near Hendricks) whipped visibility to less than a quarter mile at times through the evening of April 14. Travel was not recommended for much of the two day period, if not impossible. A storm total snowfall of 16.9 inches and drifts to several feet buried Lake Benton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet pushing across the Rockies resulted in development of low pressure across southeast Colorado late on April 2, which intensified rapidly moving across Kansas and into Illinois on April 3.  A broad swath of snow and gusty winds developed north of the low track across the area, with heaviest snowfall amounts in excess of 6 inches generally corresponding to highest terrain areas in south central and southeast South Dakota, northern portions of northwest Iowa and areas of southwest Minnesota. As unseasonably cold high pressure spread in behind the system on the morning of April 4, a few low temperature records were broken by as much as 5 to 15 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall developed after daybreak on April 3, with winds increasing substantially and gusting to 35 to 40 mph through early evening. Visibility dropped to a half mile to a mile from 0900-1700CST, creating hazardous travel conditions and a few spots with travel not recommended across southwest Minnesota.  A snowfall total of 5.5 inches was reported 4 miles north of Currie with 4.5 inches reported at Lake Wilson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most intense storm of the month, in fact many months, wrapped up across the central Plains on April 13, spreading a mix of wintry precipitation across the area, accompanied by numerous thunderstorms. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another amplifying wave in rapid west-northwest flow aloft brought a quick shot of snowfall to the region. With lapse rates slightly unstable on the warm side of the baroclinic zone, strong frontogenetic forcing resulted in a narrow band and brief of very intense snowfall which resulting in less than a half mile visibility at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall developed quickly in the morning and remained heavy at times into early afternoon. Southeast wind gusted to 30-35 mph and resulted in periods of visibility from one-quarter to one-half mile at times. Snowfall reached 6.0 inches at Worthington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late Winter storm developed across the Rockies Sunday night, March 4th, and moved out into the Plains by Monday morning. This storm continued to intensify Monday, with bands of heavy snow wrapping around the center of the storm system through Monday evening. The storm started to taper off Tuesday morning as the storm weakened and moved off to the south. ||At the beginning, a warm layer aloft kept most of the precipitation initially in the form of sleet, snow, and patchy freezing rain late Sunday night, and into Monday morning. One band of precipitation fell from southeast Minnesota, northwest into east central and central Minnesota Sunday evening, and into early Monday morning. North of the Twin Cities, the sleet and snow, changed over to all snow and became heavy at times north of St. Cloud. A quick 4 to 6 inches of snow fell in Morrison, and Todd Counties of central Minnesota during the pre-dawn hours.  ||The initial band of precipitation was very convective in nature with thunderstorms reported in southern Minnesota Monday morning. A secondary band of precipitation developed across eastern South Dakota, and eastern Nebraska. This area of precipitation moved northward, but the continued to rotate around the main storm system centered near Sioux Falls, South Dakota. The band of precipitation moved into southwest Minnesota and changed over to heavy snowfall late Monday morning. Snowfall rates increased with some areas of southwest Minnesota receiving up to 6 inches of snow by the early afternoon. This band moved into south central, southeast and east central Minnesota by the mid afternoon hours. Snowfall rates continued in the 1 to 2 inch per hour range. Slowly by the late evening, the heavier bands of snow weakened, with most areas by midnight were only reporting light snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of trained spotters, local media, and the public received locally 4 to 6 inches of snowfall during this winter storm. However, the storm also produced sleet and patchy freezing rain at times, especially early Monday morning as temperatures aloft were warm enough for the mixture of precipitation. The measurable snowfall ended late Monday evening, but light snow and flurries continued through Tuesday morning.

Southeast Minnesota was covered with 4 to 8 inches of snow on March 5th. In addition to the snow, wind gusts of 35 to 60 mph created near white-out conditions at times with lots of blowing and drifting snow. Most schools in the area dismissed early on the 5th and then had a late start on the 6th. The highest reported snow amounts were 8.1 inches near Predmore (Olmsted County) and 8.2 inches near Theilmann (Wabasha County). The highest recorded wind gust was 60 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport (Olmsted County).

Wind gusts of 40 to 45 mph created near white-out conditions at times from blowing and drifting snow. The highest recorted wind gust was 45 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Preston airport and by mesonet equipment near Canton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet pushing across the Rockies resulted in development of low pressure across southeast Colorado late on April 2, which intensified rapidly moving across Kansas and into Illinois on April 3.  A broad swath of snow and gusty winds developed north of the low track across the area, with heaviest snowfall amounts in excess of 6 inches generally corresponding to highest terrain areas in south central and southeast South Dakota, northern portions of northwest Iowa and areas of southwest Minnesota. As unseasonably cold high pressure spread in behind the system on the morning of April 4, a few low temperature records were broken by as much as 5 to 15 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall developed after daybreak on April 3, with winds increasing substantially and gusting to 35 to 40 mph through early evening. Visibility dropped to a half mile to a mile from 1100-1800CST, creating hazardous travel conditions and a few spots with travel not recommended across southwest Minnesota.  A snowfall total of 4.5 inches was reported at Worthington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another amplifying wave in rapid west-northwest flow aloft brought a quick shot of snowfall to the region. With lapse rates slightly unstable on the warm side of the baroclinic zone, strong frontogenetic forcing resulted in a narrow band and brief of very intense snowfall which resulting in less than a half mile visibility at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall developed quickly in the morning and continued briefly heavy at times into early afternoon. Southeast wind gusted to 25-35 mph and resulted in periods of visibility from one-quarter to one-half mile at times. Snowfall reached 4.0 inches at Pipestone.

This brought a prolonged period of easterly winds to eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota. East winds are generally not high impact winds, and that was the case with this storm too. Temperatures started out quite mild in the late afternoon and evening of March 4th, with most locations in the mid to upper 30s. Rain showers, with isolated thunder, developed over southeast North Dakota by the late afternoon, then spread north-northeast during the evening. As this activity lifted north-northeast, it changed over to freezing rain, sleet, and snow. During the early morning hours of the 5th, intense bands of snow developed over the southern Red River Valley and lifted north-northwest. Snowfall rates within these bands were about one to three inches per hour, from the Fargo-Moorhead area up toward Mayville and Northwood. Areas of freezing rain or mixed precipitation also fell in other areas too. However, for most of the daytime hours of the 5th, the precipitation type remained snow. Within the broad area of snow, intense thin snow bands remained. Therefore, snow totals remained highly variable, even across short distances.

A long-duration early Spring storm dropped several inches of snow from Monday, April 2nd, through late Tuesday night, April 3rd. ||The first part of the storm developed across southern Minnesota Monday morning, and drifted northward, and affected areas along and north of Highway 212 during the afternoon, and evening. Locally 3 to 6 inches fell from Alexandria, to St. Cloud, Minnesota. ||The second part of the storm developed across the Central Plains early Tuesday morning, and rapidly expanded northeast across the Upper Midwest. ||Local radar and surface observations indicated that two waves of snow occurred with the second part of the storm. The first wave affected south central Minnesota, into west central Wisconsin Tuesday morning. The second wave moved across southwest, into south central, east central Minnesota, and eventually into west central Wisconsin from Tuesday afternoon through Tuesday evening.||By the time all the snow ended, snowfall amounts ranged from 3-6 inches across west central and southwest Minnesota, to 4-8 inches across central, east central, and south central Minnesota. Locally higher amounts were noted around the Twin Cities Metro area, St. Cloud and Dassel, Minnesota which is 100 miles west of the Twin Cities. ||Two day totals were quite impressive for early April with the Minneapolis/St. Paul Airport recording a daily snowfall record of 9.0 inches on April 3rd. ||Other reported snowfall amounts include;||10.6 inches near downtown Minneapolis.|10.2 inches near Dassel.|9.7 inches at the NWS Office in Chanhassen. |8.7 inches near Lakeville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two storm systems produced several rounds of snowfall across Blue Earth County. The last round of snow ended Tuesday evening and amounts ranged from 5 to 8 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged snow storm event with historic snowfall totals for the month of April, started Friday, April 13th, and lasted until late Sunday night April 15th. ||This event was split into several different precipitation hazards, which created concerns with communicating the impacts of sleet, snow, blowing snow and blizzard conditions. As the event unfolded, this storm began apparent that it would produce record snowfall amounts, along with the highest snowfall amounts so far into the month of April. Therefore, the wording changed from significant/severe winter storm, to historic. ||The first part of the storm developed early Friday morning with bands of showers, and embedded thunderstorms moving northward along the Iowa border. Severe storms were noted south of the Iowa border, with one report of severe winds that occurred in far southwest Minnesota near Magnolia. These storms moved northward into colder air aloft and produced widespread areas of sleet, small hail, but no severe storms. However, there were numerous reports across southern Minnesota where the small hail, and sleet accumulated up to 2 inches in depth. ||Several automated airport observing sites (ASOS/AWOS) reported heavy rainfall, sleet and thunderstorms through the morning, and into the afternoon for the southern 1/4 of the state. By the late morning, and into the afternoon, surface temperatures cooled in west central Minnesota where the precipitation changed over to snow.  As the precipitation moved further north into central Minnesota, the precipitation changed over to snow. A few locations reported moderate to heavy snowfall where locally 2 to 4 inches fell in a short period of time west of St. Cloud, Minnesota. ||The next round of precipitation was in the form of sleet and heavy rain in far southern Minnesota, with snow to the north in the evening. Most of this area of precipitation quickly moved off to the east, with a lull period until early Saturday morning with the main bands of heavy snowfall developed in southwest, and into west central and east central Minnesota by noon. ||Total amounts through Saturday morning ranged from locally 6 inches in west central Minnesota, to an average of 1 to 3 inches from Redwood Falls through the Twin Cities, and into west central Wisconsin near Eau Claire. There was also a band of 2 to 4 inches that fell early Friday afternoon in central Minnesota. South of the Minnesota River valley, only trace amounts were noted, with some icy spots due to overnight sleet and patchy freezing rain. ||By late Saturday morning, and into Saturday afternoon, the snowfall intensified and areal coverage widened across southern and central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were common by the afternoon before the heaviest snowfall began to taper off late Saturday evening. ||The second round, and the third round of precipitation, occurred with the main storm system moving across Iowa and into the Great Lakes region late Saturday night, through late Sunday evening. After Saturday afternoon, snowfall intensity varied with some waves of heavy snowfall from Saturday night, and into Friday afternoon, but the majority of the snow was light.||The worst conditions occurred early Saturday morning, through Saturday afternoon in terms of high end impacts. Strong northeast winds developed in the morning and held in the 25 to 35 mph range, with gusts of 40 to 50 mph through the afternoon and early evening. At times, surface visibility was only a few hundred feet in open country.  The strong winds also combined with the higher snowfall rates, to create blizzard conditions across most of southern Minnesota, west of Interstate 35. Even the inner core of the Twin Cities had visibility so low and strong winds that a blizzard warning was issued. This was the first blizzard warning issued for the Twin Cities Metro area since the blizzard of April 14, 1983. This blizzard grounded flights at Minneapolis-St. Paul International Airport for over seven hours Saturday, caused over 600 car accidents, nearly 1200 spin-outs, and approximately 20 jackknifed semi trailers. The storm also collapsed the 77-foot-high dome of the Vadnais Sports Center, and forced three consecutive Minnesota Twins home games to be postponed for the first time since Target Field opened. The storm caused the Minnesota Department of Transportation to close several county roads in west central, southwest and south central Minnesota Saturday morning, with Interstate 90 closed by noon, west of Blue Earth. ||From Friday through Sunday morning, According to the state patrol, more than 50 injuries were reported from Friday through Sunday morning, two of which were listed as serious. One death occurred when a man in Medina ran across a highway and was struck by a vehicle.||Conditions slowly improved during the evening, but bands of snow continued. The wind speeds continued to weaken, but strong enough to create areas of blowing snow, with brief periods of whiteout condition in open country.||As the storm ended, the following are some records from this storm. ||15.8 inches of snowfall at the Minneapolis/St. Paul Airport. This was the heaviest April snowstorm on record. It was also the 12th-heaviest snowstorm on record for any month. Plus, the airport had individual maximum snowfall totals on the day on the 15th and 16th. Additionally, by holding temperatures to the 20s or very low 30s during Saturday and Sunday, the storm enabled St. Cloud, and the Twin Cities to break records for the lowest high temperature of the day.||The following snowfall totals were the highest in the state. ||21.4 inches near Madison.|20.5 inches near Victoria. |20.5 inches near Montevideo.|20.2 inches near North St. Paul.|20.0 inches near Dawson.|19.9 inches near downtown Minneapolis.|19.6 inches near St Bonifacius.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the winter storm intensified Saturday morning, winds increased to 25 to 30 mph, with gusts above 40 mph in the late morning, and afternoon. Visibility was very poor and numerous accidents occurred, along with cars stuck in the snow across the county. Conditions slowly improved during the evening as wind speeds lowered and the snowfall intensity decreased.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeast Minnesota was covered with 6 to 9 inches of snow on April 3rd as an early spring snow storm moved across the region.  Impacts from the snow were rather limited as it mainly fell during the daytime allowing melting to occur on most roads. Some schools did close for the day on the 3rd with several having late starts on the 4th. The highest reported total was 9 inches near La Crescent (Houston County), near Wabasha (Wabasha County) and in Plainview (Wabasha County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across Mower County. The highest reported total was 8 inches near Elkton and in Grand Meadow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeast Minnesota received between 5 and 12 inches of snow as another early spring storm moved across the Upper Midwest from April 13th into the 15th. In addition to the falling snow, additional impacts occurred as the wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph produced blowing and drifting snow that significantly reduced the visibility at times. The highest reported snowfall total was 12 inches in Lake City (Wabasha County) and the highest recorded wind gust was 55 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that around 5 inches of snow fell across Dodge County. Wind gusts of 40 to 45 mph created additional impacts with blowing and drifting snow that significantly reduced the visibility at times. The highest recorded wind gust was 47 mph by a mesonet station east of Kasson.

Impacts from the snow were rather limited as it mainly fell during the daytime allowing melting to occur on most roads. Some schools did close for the day on the 3rd with several having late starts on the 4th. The highest reported total was 9 inches near La Crescent (Houston County), near Wabasha (Wabasha County) and in Plainview (Wabasha County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 6 to 8 inches of snow fell across Winona County. The highest reported total was 8 inches in Minnesota City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was an unusually strong mid-April snowstorm. The area of low pressure tracked  slowly from the Central Plains to south of the Great Lakes before lifting into eastern Canada. It brought heavy snow as far north and west as northeast MN. Snow first spread in force Saturday (April 14th) afternoon into northwest WI and east-central MN from the southwest. The snow then spread into the rest of the Northland Saturday night into Sunday. The snow continued much of Sunday, then slowly diminished Sunday night into Monday. Snowfall amounts ranged from a trace in western areas of the CWA, to a little over a foot in parts of southern St. Louis County.|This storm generated strong northeast winds over Lake Superior. Park Point in Duluth had frequent gusts to 50 mph Friday night and Saturday. The winds generated large waves of at least up to 15 feet over Lake Superior and along the shore from the Duluth area and along the north shore. There was some flooding near the ship canal and Lift Bridge in Duluth from the waves, and the waves were high|enough to hit the bridge deck. Since it was cold, the waves and spray created a lot of icing along the Lake Superior shore. The high surf along the Minnesota shore of Lake Superior in Duluth led to coastal flooding and heavy damage to the lakewalk in Duluth. Damage estimates are in the $9 million price range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third spring storm of April dropped a band of heavy snow on portions of southeast Minnesota on April 18th. Amounts of 4 to 12 inches were reported generally south of Interstate 90. The highest reported total was 12 inches in Le Roy (Mower County). With snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches, roads quickly became snow covered and slippery, but given the time of year, melting started soon after the snow stopped and the roads were promptly cleared.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 4 to 10 inches of snow fell across Fillmore County. The highest reported total was 10 inches in Harmony.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was an unusually strong mid-April snowstorm. The area of low pressure tracked  slowly from the Central Plains to south of the Great Lakes before lifting into eastern Canada. It brought heavy snow as far north and west as northeast MN. Snow first spread in force Saturday (April 14th) afternoon into northwest WI and east-central MN from the southwest. The snow then spread into the rest of the Northland Saturday night into Sunday. The snow continued much of Sunday, then slowly diminished Sunday night into Monday. Snowfall amounts ranged from a trace in western areas of the CWA, to a little over a foot in parts of southern St. Louis County.|This storm generated strong northeast winds over Lake Superior. Park Point in Duluth had frequent gusts to 50 mph Friday night and Saturday. The winds generated large waves of at least up to 15 feet over Lake Superior and along the shore from the Duluth area and along the north shore. There was some flooding near the ship canal and Lift Bridge in Duluth from the waves, and the waves were high|enough to hit the bridge deck. Since it was cold, the waves and spray created a lot of icing along the Lake Superior shore. The high surf along the Minnesota shore of Lake Superior in Duluth led to coastal flooding and heavy damage to the lakewalk in Duluth. Damage estimates are in the $9 million price range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged snow storm event with historic snowfall totals for the month of April, started Friday, April 13th, and lasted until late Sunday night April 15th. ||This event was split into several different precipitation hazards, which created concerns with communicating the impacts of sleet, snow, blowing snow and blizzard conditions. As the event unfolded, this storm began apparent that it would produce record snowfall amounts, along with the highest snowfall amounts so far into the month of April. Therefore, the wording changed from significant/severe winter storm, to historic. ||The first part of the storm developed early Friday morning with bands of showers, and embedded thunderstorms moving northward along the Iowa border. Severe storms were noted south of the Iowa border, with one report of severe winds that occurred in far southwest Minnesota near Magnolia. These storms moved northward into colder air aloft and produced widespread areas of sleet, small hail, but no severe storms. However, there were numerous reports across southern Minnesota where the small hail, and sleet accumulated up to 2 inches in depth. ||Several automated airport observing sites (ASOS/AWOS) reported heavy rainfall, sleet and thunderstorms through the morning, and into the afternoon for the southern 1/4 of the state. By the late morning, and into the afternoon, surface temperatures cooled in west central Minnesota where the precipitation changed over to snow.  As the precipitation moved further north into central Minnesota, the precipitation changed over to snow. A few locations reported moderate to heavy snowfall where locally 2 to 4 inches fell in a short period of time west of St. Cloud, Minnesota. ||The next round of precipitation was in the form of sleet and heavy rain in far southern Minnesota, with snow to the north in the evening. Most of this area of precipitation quickly moved off to the east, with a lull period until early Saturday morning with the main bands of heavy snowfall developed in southwest, and into west central and east central Minnesota by noon. ||Total amounts through Saturday morning ranged from locally 6 inches in west central Minnesota, to an average of 1 to 3 inches from Redwood Falls through the Twin Cities, and into west central Wisconsin near Eau Claire. There was also a band of 2 to 4 inches that fell early Friday afternoon in central Minnesota. South of the Minnesota River valley, only trace amounts were noted, with some icy spots due to overnight sleet and patchy freezing rain. ||By late Saturday morning, and into Saturday afternoon, the snowfall intensified and areal coverage widened across southern and central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were common by the afternoon before the heaviest snowfall began to taper off late Saturday evening. ||The second round, and the third round of precipitation, occurred with the main storm system moving across Iowa and into the Great Lakes region late Saturday night, through late Sunday evening. After Saturday afternoon, snowfall intensity varied with some waves of heavy snowfall from Saturday night, and into Friday afternoon, but the majority of the snow was light.||The worst conditions occurred early Saturday morning, through Saturday afternoon in terms of high end impacts. Strong northeast winds developed in the morning and held in the 25 to 35 mph range, with gusts of 40 to 50 mph through the afternoon and early evening. At times, surface visibility was only a few hundred feet in open country.  The strong winds also combined with the higher snowfall rates, to create blizzard conditions across most of southern Minnesota, west of Interstate 35. Even the inner core of the Twin Cities had visibility so low and strong winds that a blizzard warning was issued. This was the first blizzard warning issued for the Twin Cities Metro area since the blizzard of April 14, 1983. This blizzard grounded flights at Minneapolis-St. Paul International Airport for over seven hours Saturday, caused over 600 car accidents, nearly 1200 spin-outs, and approximately 20 jackknifed semi trailers. The storm also collapsed the 77-foot-high dome of the Vadnais Sports Center, and forced three consecutive Minnesota Twins home games to be postponed for the first time since Target Field opened. The storm caused the Minnesota Department of Transportation to close several county roads in west central, southwest and south central Minnesota Saturday morning, with Interstate 90 closed by noon, west of Blue Earth. ||From Friday through Sunday morning, According to the state patrol, more than 50 injuries were reported from Friday through Sunday morning, two of which were listed as serious. One death occurred when a man in Medina ran across a highway and was struck by a vehicle.||Conditions slowly improved during the evening, but bands of snow continued. The wind speeds continued to weaken, but strong enough to create areas of blowing snow, with brief periods of whiteout condition in open country.||As the storm ended, the following are some records from this storm. ||15.8 inches of snowfall at the Minneapolis/St. Paul Airport. This was the heaviest April snowstorm on record. It was also the 12th-heaviest snowstorm on record for any month. Plus, the airport had individual maximum snowfall totals on the day on the 15th and 16th. Additionally, by holding temperatures to the 20s or very low 30s during Saturday and Sunday, the storm enabled St. Cloud, and the Twin Cities to break records for the lowest high temperature of the day.||The following snowfall totals were the highest in the state. ||21.4 inches near Madison.|20.5 inches near Victoria. |20.5 inches near Montevideo.|20.2 inches near North St. Paul.|20.0 inches near Dawson.|19.9 inches near downtown Minneapolis.|19.6 inches near St Bonifacius.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were two main waves of snow that occurred across Stearns County. The first developed Friday afternoon April 13th, and produced a few inches across the county. By the evening, most of the snow tapered off. By Saturday morning, another round of heavier snowfall developed and by Sunday morning, 6 to 8 inches of snow fell in mainly the southern part of the county. There were a few isolated locations near Kimball that received 12 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeast Minnesota was covered with 6 to 9 inches of snow on April 3rd as an early spring snow storm moved across the region.  Impacts from the snow were rather limited as it mainly fell during the daytime allowing melting to occur on most roads. Some schools did close for the day on the 3rd with several having late starts on the 4th. The highest reported total was 9 inches near La Crescent (Houston County), near Wabasha (Wabasha County) and in Plainview (Wabasha County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 6 to 9 inches of snow fell across Houston County. The highest reported total was 9 inches near La Crescent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of the Redwood River basin received from one to two inches of liquid equivalent in the week leading up to the minor flooding, including snow melt moisture from the historic blizzard on April 14 and subsequent snowfall less than a week later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and runoff from periods of heavier rainfall briefly produced moderate flooding with impact to lowland agricultural areas. River levels reached 3.3 feet above flood stage on the Redwood River at Russell (gage site RUSM5) on April 24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snowmelt caused some southeast Minnesota river flooding to occur in late April along the Mississippi River. The flooding continued into early May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snowmelt pushed the Mississippi River out of its banks in Wabasha. The flooding continued into early May which is when the crest occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved from the northern Rockies, southeast across the Northern Plains, and Upper Midwest, on Sunday, April 8th. Most of the snowfall, with amounts of 3-5 inches, fell along and south of the Minnesota River Valley during the late morning, and afternoon. The heaviest fell in South Dakota, and far northern Iowa where a few areas received up to 9 inches of snow. A small portion of west central Minnesota near Canby and Madison, Minnesota, received between 6 to 8 inches of snow from the late morning, through the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few areas received up to 7 inches of snow around Dawson from this storm.
