However, there were numerous reports across southern Minnesota where the small hail, and sleet accumulated up to 2 inches in depth. ||Several automated airport observing sites (ASOS/AWOS) reported heavy rainfall, sleet and thunderstorms through the morning, and into the afternoon for the southern 1/4 of the state. By the late morning, and into the afternoon, surface temperatures cooled in west central Minnesota where the precipitation changed over to snow.  As the precipitation moved further north into central Minnesota, the precipitation changed over to snow. A few locations reported moderate to heavy snowfall where locally 2 to 4 inches fell in a short period of time west of St. Cloud, Minnesota. ||The next round of precipitation was in the form of sleet and heavy rain in far southern Minnesota, with snow to the north in the evening. Most of this area of precipitation quickly moved off to the east, with a lull period until early Saturday morning with the main bands of heavy snowfall developed in southwest, and into west central and east central Minnesota by noon. ||Total amounts through Saturday morning ranged from locally 6 inches in west central Minnesota, to an average of 1 to 3 inches from Redwood Falls through the Twin Cities, and into west central Wisconsin near Eau Claire. There was also a band of 2 to 4 inches that fell early Friday afternoon in central Minnesota. South of the Minnesota River valley, only trace amounts were noted, with some icy spots due to overnight sleet and patchy freezing rain. ||By late Saturday morning, and into Saturday afternoon, the snowfall intensified and areal coverage widened across southern and central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were common by the afternoon before the heaviest snowfall began to taper off late Saturday evening. ||The second round, and the third round of precipitation, occurred with the main storm system moving across Iowa and into the Great Lakes region late Saturday night, through late Sunday evening. After Saturday afternoon, snowfall intensity varied with some waves of heavy snowfall from Saturday night, and into Friday afternoon, but the majority of the snow was light.||The worst conditions occurred early Saturday morning, through Saturday afternoon in terms of high end impacts. Strong northeast winds developed in the morning and held in the 25 to 35 mph range, with gusts of 40 to 50 mph through the afternoon and early evening. At times, surface visibility was only a few hundred feet in open country.  The strong winds also combined with the higher snowfall rates, to create blizzard conditions across most of southern Minnesota, west of Interstate 35. Even the inner core of the Twin Cities had visibility so low and strong winds that a blizzard warning was issued. This was the first blizzard warning issued for the Twin Cities Metro area since the blizzard of April 14, 1983. This blizzard grounded flights at Minneapolis-St. Paul International Airport for over seven hours Saturday, caused over 600 car accidents, nearly 1200 spin-outs, and approximately 20 jackknifed semi trailers. The storm also collapsed the 77-foot-high dome of the Vadnais Sports Center, and forced three consecutive Minnesota Twins home games to be postponed for the first time since Target Field opened. The heaviest snowfall with rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour occurred Saturday morning. The snow tapered off early Sunday morning. Total snowfall amounts by Monday morning ranged from 18 to 21 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small but strong winter storm moved across Iowa Wednesday, April 18th, and produced a swath of 6 to locally 11 inches of snow from Fairmont, to Albert Lea. ||During the morning of Wednesday, April 18th, light snow began in far southern Minnesota, near the Iowa border, and slowly spread north and east during the day. EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several reports of snowfall totals between 6 and 10 across the county. The highest total was in Bricelyn who reported 9.8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of the Rock River basin received from one to two inches of liquid equivalent in the week leading up to the minor flooding, including snow melt moisture from the historic blizzard on April 14.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and runoff from periods of heavier rainfall briefly produced minor flooding with impact to lowland agricultural areas. River levels reached 0.5 feet above flood stage on the Rock River near Hardwick (gage site HRDM5) on April 24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of the Redwood River basin received from one to two inches of liquid equivalent in the week leading up to the minor flooding, including snow melt moisture from the historic blizzard on April 14 and subsequent snowfall less than a week later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and runoff from periods of heavier rainfall briefly produced moderate flooding with impact to lowland agricultural areas. River levels reached 1.9 feet above flood stage on the Redwood River at Marshall (gage site MSHM5) on April 24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snowmelt caused some southeast Minnesota river flooding to occur in late April along the Mississippi River. The flooding continued into early May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from snowmelt pushed the Mississippi River out of its banks in Winona. The flooding continued into early May which is when the crest occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeast Minnesota was covered with 6 to 9 inches of snow on April 3rd as an early spring snow storm moved across the region.  Impacts from the snow were rather limited as it mainly fell during the daytime allowing melting to occur on most roads. Some schools did close for the day on the 3rd with several having late starts on the 4th. The highest reported total was 9 inches near La Crescent (Houston County), near Wabasha (Wabasha County) and in Plainview (Wabasha County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 6 to 8 inches of snow fell across Olmsted County. The highest reported total was 8.1 inches near Elgin. 11 accidents were reported across the county and there was one injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged snow storm event with historic snowfall totals for the month of April, started Friday, April 13th, and lasted until late Sunday night April 15th. ||This event was split into several different precipitation hazards, which created concerns with communicating the impacts of sleet, snow, blowing snow and blizzard conditions. As the event unfolded, this storm began apparent that it would produce record snowfall amounts, along with the highest snowfall amounts so far into the month of April. Therefore, the wording changed from significant/severe winter storm, to historic. ||The first part of the storm developed early Friday morning with bands of showers, and embedded thunderstorms moving northward along the Iowa border. Severe storms were noted south of the Iowa border, with one report of severe winds that occurred in far southwest Minnesota near Magnolia. These storms moved northward into colder air aloft and produced widespread areas of sleet, small hail, but no severe storms. However, there were numerous reports across southern Minnesota where the small hail, and sleet accumulated up to 2 inches in depth. ||Several automated airport observing sites (ASOS/AWOS) reported heavy rainfall, sleet and thunderstorms through the morning, and into the afternoon for the southern 1/4 of the state. By the late morning, and into the afternoon, surface temperatures cooled in west central Minnesota where the precipitation changed over to snow.  As the precipitation moved further north into central Minnesota, the precipitation changed over to snow. A few locations reported moderate to heavy snowfall where locally 2 to 4 inches fell in a short period of time west of St. Cloud, Minnesota. ||The next round of precipitation was in the form of sleet and heavy rain in far southern Minnesota, with snow to the north in the evening. Most of this area of precipitation quickly moved off to the east, with a lull period until early Saturday morning with the main bands of heavy snowfall developed in southwest, and into west central and east central Minnesota by noon. ||Total amounts through Saturday morning ranged from locally 6 inches in west central Minnesota, to an average of 1 to 3 inches from Redwood Falls through the Twin Cities, and into west central Wisconsin near Eau Claire. There was also a band of 2 to 4 inches that fell early Friday afternoon in central Minnesota. South of the Minnesota River valley, only trace amounts were noted, with some icy spots due to overnight sleet and patchy freezing rain. ||By late Saturday morning, and into Saturday afternoon, the snowfall intensified and areal coverage widened across southern and central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were common by the afternoon before the heaviest snowfall began to taper off late Saturday evening. ||The second round, and the third round of precipitation, occurred with the main storm system moving across Iowa and into the Great Lakes region late Saturday night, through late Sunday evening. After Saturday afternoon, snowfall intensity varied with some waves of heavy snowfall from Saturday night, and into Friday afternoon, but the majority of the snow was light.||The worst conditions occurred early Saturday morning, through Saturday afternoon in terms of high end impacts. Strong northeast winds developed in the morning and held in the 25 to 35 mph range, with gusts of 40 to 50 mph through the afternoon and early evening. At times, surface visibility was only a few hundred feet in open country.  The strong winds also combined with the higher snowfall rates, to create blizzard conditions across most of southern Minnesota, west of Interstate 35. Even the inner core of the Twin Cities had visibility so low and strong winds that a blizzard warning was issued. This was the first blizzard warning issued for the Twin Cities Metro area since the blizzard of April 14, 1983. This blizzard grounded flights at Minneapolis-St. Paul International Airport for over seven hours Saturday, caused over 600 car accidents, nearly 1200 spin-outs, and approximately 20 jackknifed semi trailers. The storm also collapsed the 77-foot-high dome of the Vadnais Sports Center, and forced three consecutive Minnesota Twins home games to be postponed for the first time since Target Field opened. The storm caused the Minnesota Department of Transportation to close several county roads in west central, southwest and south central Minnesota Saturday morning, with Interstate 90 closed by noon, west of Blue Earth. ||From Friday through Sunday morning, According to the state patrol, more than 50 injuries were reported from Friday through Sunday morning, two of which were listed as serious. One death occurred when a man in Medina ran across a highway and was struck by a vehicle.||Conditions slowly improved during the evening, but bands of snow continued. The wind speeds continued to weaken, but strong enough to create areas of blowing snow, with brief periods of whiteout condition in open country.||As the storm ended, the following are some records from this storm. ||15.8 inches of snowfall at the Minneapolis/St. Paul Airport. This was the heaviest April snowstorm on record. It was also the 12th-heaviest snowstorm on record for any month. Plus, the airport had individual maximum snowfall totals on the day on the 15th and 16th. Additionally, by holding temperatures to the 20s or very low 30s during Saturday and Sunday, the storm enabled St. Cloud, and the Twin Cities to break records for the lowest high temperature of the day.||The following snowfall totals were the highest in the state. ||21.4 inches near Madison.|20.5 inches near Victoria. |20.5 inches near Montevideo.|20.2 inches near North St. Paul.|20.0 inches near Dawson.|19.9 inches near downtown Minneapolis.|19.6 inches near St Bonifacius.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several waves of precipitation occurred across Faribault County between early Saturday morning, through Sunday morning. Although some sleet, small hail and thunder occurred Friday morning, the bulk of the snow started early Saturday. ||The first wave developed Saturday morning before noon and produced some sleet and snow, along with thundersnow. The next batch of heavy snowfall developed Saturday afternoon, into Saturday evening. The snow tapered off Sunday morning. Total snowfall amounts by Monday morning ranged from 6 to 14 inches.

Total snowfall amounts by Monday morning ranged from 6 to 12 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several reports of snowfall totals between 6 and 11 across the county. The highest total was in Glenville who reported 11.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of the Redwood River basin received from one to two inches of liquid equivalent in the week leading up to the minor flooding, including snow melt moisture from the historic blizzard on April 14 and subsequent snowfall less than a week later.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and runoff from periods of heavier rainfall briefly produced moderate flooding with impact to lowland agricultural areas. River levels reached 3.3 feet above flood stage on the Redwood River at Russell (gage site RUSM5) on April 24.

As the event unfolded, this storm began apparent that it would produce record snowfall amounts, along with the highest snowfall amounts so far into the month of April. Therefore, the wording changed from significant/severe winter storm, to historic. ||The first part of the storm developed early Friday morning with bands of showers, and embedded thunderstorms moving northward along the Iowa border. Severe storms were noted south of the Iowa border, with one report of severe winds that occurred in far southwest Minnesota near Magnolia. These storms moved northward into colder air aloft and produced widespread areas of sleet, small hail, but no severe storms. However, there were numerous reports across southern Minnesota where the small hail, and sleet accumulated up to 2 inches in depth. ||Several automated airport observing sites (ASOS/AWOS) reported heavy rainfall, sleet and thunderstorms through the morning, and into the afternoon for the southern 1/4 of the state. By the late morning, and into the afternoon, surface temperatures cooled in west central Minnesota where the precipitation changed over to snow.  As the precipitation moved further north into central Minnesota, the precipitation changed over to snow. A few locations reported moderate to heavy snowfall where locally 2 to 4 inches fell in a short period of time west of St. Cloud, Minnesota. ||The next round of precipitation was in the form of sleet and heavy rain in far southern Minnesota, with snow to the north in the evening. Most of this area of precipitation quickly moved off to the east, with a lull period until early Saturday morning with the main bands of heavy snowfall developed in southwest, and into west central and east central Minnesota by noon. ||Total amounts through Saturday morning ranged from locally 6 inches in west central Minnesota, to an average of 1 to 3 inches from Redwood Falls through the Twin Cities, and into west central Wisconsin near Eau Claire. There was also a band of 2 to 4 inches that fell early Friday afternoon in central Minnesota. South of the Minnesota River valley, only trace amounts were noted, with some icy spots due to overnight sleet and patchy freezing rain. ||By late Saturday morning, and into Saturday afternoon, the snowfall intensified and areal coverage widened across southern and central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour were common by the afternoon before the heaviest snowfall began to taper off late Saturday evening. ||The second round, and the third round of precipitation, occurred with the main storm system moving across Iowa and into the Great Lakes region late Saturday night, through late Sunday evening. After Saturday afternoon, snowfall intensity varied with some waves of heavy snowfall from Saturday night, and into Friday afternoon, but the majority of the snow was light.||The worst conditions occurred early Saturday morning, through Saturday afternoon in terms of high end impacts. Strong northeast winds developed in the morning and held in the 25 to 35 mph range, with gusts of 40 to 50 mph through the afternoon and early evening. At times, surface visibility was only a few hundred feet in open country.  The strong winds also combined with the higher snowfall rates, to create blizzard conditions across most of southern Minnesota, west of Interstate 35. Even the inner core of the Twin Cities had visibility so low and strong winds that a blizzard warning was issued. This was the first blizzard warning issued for the Twin Cities Metro area since the blizzard of April 14, 1983. This blizzard grounded flights at Minneapolis-St. Paul International Airport for over seven hours Saturday, caused over 600 car accidents, nearly 1200 spin-outs, and approximately 20 jackknifed semi trailers. The storm also collapsed the 77-foot-high dome of the Vadnais Sports Center, and forced three consecutive Minnesota Twins home games to be postponed for the first time since Target Field opened. The storm caused the Minnesota Department of Transportation to close several county roads in west central, southwest and south central Minnesota Saturday morning, with Interstate 90 closed by noon, west of Blue Earth. ||From Friday through Sunday morning, According to the state patrol, more than 50 injuries were reported from Friday through Sunday morning, two of which were listed as serious. One death occurred when a man in Medina ran across a highway and was struck by a vehicle.||Conditions slowly improved during the evening, but bands of snow continued. The wind speeds continued to weaken, but strong enough to create areas of blowing snow, with brief periods of whiteout condition in open country.||As the storm ended, the following are some records from this storm. ||15.8 inches of snowfall at the Minneapolis/St. Paul Airport. This was the heaviest April snowstorm on record. It was also the 12th-heaviest snowstorm on record for any month. Plus, the airport had individual maximum snowfall totals on the day on the 15th and 16th. Additionally, by holding temperatures to the 20s or very low 30s during Saturday and Sunday, the storm enabled St. Cloud, and the Twin Cities to break records for the lowest high temperature of the day.||The following snowfall totals were the highest in the state. ||21.4 inches near Madison.|20.5 inches near Victoria. |20.5 inches near Montevideo.|20.2 inches near North St. Paul.|20.0 inches near Dawson.|19.9 inches near downtown Minneapolis.|19.6 inches near St Bonifacius.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged snow storm event with historic snowfall totals for the month of April, started Friday, April 13th, and lasted until late Sunday night April 15th. ||This event was split into several different precipitation hazards, which created concerns with communicating the impacts of sleet, snow, blowing snow and blizzard conditions. Severe storms were noted south of the Iowa border, with one report of severe winds that occurred in far southwest Minnesota near Magnolia. These storms moved northward into colder air aloft and produced widespread areas of sleet, small hail, but no severe storms. However, there were numerous reports across southern Minnesota where the small hail, and sleet accumulated up to 2 inches in depth. ||Several automated airport observing sites (ASOS/AWOS) reported heavy rainfall, sleet and thunderstorms through the morning, and into the afternoon for the southern 1/4 of the state. By the late morning, and into the afternoon, surface temperatures cooled in west central Minnesota where the precipitation changed over to snow.  As the precipitation moved further north into central Minnesota, the precipitation changed over to snow. A few locations reported moderate to heavy snowfall where locally 2 to 4 inches fell in a short period of time west of St. Cloud, Minnesota. ||The next round of precipitation was in the form of sleet and heavy rain in far southern Minnesota, with snow to the north in the evening. Most of this area of precipitation quickly moved off to the east, with a lull period until early Saturday morning with the main bands of heavy snowfall developed in southwest, and into west central and east central Minnesota by noon. ||Total amounts through Saturday morning ranged from locally 6 inches in west central Minnesota, to an average of 1 to 3 inches from Redwood Falls through the Twin Cities, and into west central Wisconsin near Eau Claire. There was also a band of 2 to 4 inches that fell early Friday afternoon in central Minnesota. South of the Minnesota River valley, only trace amounts were noted, with some icy spots due to overnight sleet and patchy freezing rain. ||By late Saturday morning, and into Saturday afternoon, the snowfall intensified and areal coverage widened across southern and central Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin. Strong northeast winds developed in the morning and held in the 25 to 35 mph range, with gusts of 40 to 50 mph through the afternoon and early evening. At times, surface visibility was only a few hundred feet in open country.  The strong winds also combined with the higher snowfall rates, to create blizzard conditions across most of southern Minnesota, west of Interstate 35. Even the inner core of the Twin Cities had visibility so low and strong winds that a blizzard warning was issued. This blizzard grounded flights at Minneapolis-St. Paul International Airport for over seven hours Saturday, caused over 600 car accidents, nearly 1200 spin-outs, and approximately 20 jackknifed semi trailers. One death occurred when a man in Medina ran across a highway and was struck by a vehicle.||Conditions slowly improved during the evening, but bands of snow continued. The wind speeds continued to weaken, but strong enough to create areas of blowing snow, with brief periods of whiteout condition in open country.||As the storm ended, the following are some records from this storm. ||15.8 inches of snowfall at the Minneapolis/St. Paul Airport. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front pushed across northeastern Minnesota during the afternoon hours leading to a few isolated storms. One of the storms was more robust than the rest and produced some severe hail near Lake Vermilion in northern St. Louis County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A restaurant at the far west end of Lake Vermilion reported mostly dime-sized hail with a few larger stones to the size of quarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was draped across the southern and southwestern reaches of the CWA during the day. By mid-afternoon, daytime heating led to two supercells developing along the warm front and traveling to the east-southeast along the front. Large hail and numerous trees were reported down, including quite a few trees in the Barnum, Minnesota, area from what appeared to be a microburst according to radar and damage photos.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail fell for about 5 minutes along with heavy rainfall.

The third and final day of a three day severe weather event across the Northland. A stalled out frontal system finally made a push across the area leading to another round of severe storms and heavy rainfall over areas that had seen dramatic flash flooding the previous night. Widespread rainfall amounts of 1-2 inches plus over northeastern Minnesota and northwestern Wisconsin sent already swollen creeks and rivers out of their banks and over roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One hundred feet of roof on a barn was ripped off by the wind. Several large trees were also blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The early morning hours of Friday, June 29th, remained warm and muggy. Just after midnight, temperatures were still in the 70s with dew points around 70. A line of thunderstorms moved into the Devils Lake area around 2 am CDT. This line of storms had several bowing segments, which are indicative of strong winds. The most obvious bowing segment between highway 2 and Interstate 94, where the most amount of damage occurred. The main bowing segment exited into north central Minnesota around 6 am CDT. Well behind the bowing thunderstorm complex, a mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) formed, and produced another round of widespread damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was likely wrapped in downburst winds and heavy rain for most of its path. It tracked nearly due east at about 60 mph, for at least 15 miles. It tore down trees in shelterbelts and tore roofing off of a couple of homes and farm buildings. It tore down 11 miles, or more than 50 high voltage towers, and snapped wooden power poles at cross roads along its path. Peak winds were estimated at 120 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms began in western North Dakota early in the evening of June 28th and then swept into northern Minnesota during the early morning hours of the 29th. While there were a few hail reports, this line was primarily a damaging wind producer and featured a measured gust to 80 mph. Numerous trees were damaged by the storms and fell onto homes and power lines.The storms continued to produce strong winds over the open waters of Lake Superior, but weakened to the south as they moved into northwestern Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto a home and took down a power line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving into the Red River Valley along with attendant warm and cold fronts, led to the development of storms in eastern North Dakota and western Minnesota the evening of the 29th. These storms then pushed east south of the warm front across northern Minnesota during the early morning hours of the 30th. The line of storms featured a bow echo leading to numerous wind damage reports. Lightning also struck a cabin injuring a person sleeping inside the cabin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large trees were blown down with some uprooted and snapped. A shed was damaged. The wind damage extended a couple miles northeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was draped across the southern and southwestern reaches of the CWA during the day. By mid-afternoon, daytime heating led to two supercells developing along the warm front and traveling to the east-southeast along the front. Large hail and numerous trees were reported down, including quite a few trees in the Barnum, Minnesota, area from what appeared to be a microburst according to radar and damage photos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Primed by 1 to 3 inches of rainfall on June 17-18, starting June 20, the area was impacted adversely by several very slow moving mid-level waves which drifted across the northern and central Plains during the following week. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A week of periodic heavy rainfall ending June 26 resulted in a widespread 4 to 8 inches of rainfall, much of which occurred on June 20 and 24. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was located across central Minnesota into southwest Wisconsin. Low level flow maintained the influx of moisture and warm air as a shortwave topped a ridge in place to the west. The storms also contained heavy rainfall which primed parts of the area for major flash flooding the following night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A foot of water covered the roadway near the intersection of S 27th Ave West and West Michigan Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across South Dakota during the evening of Friday, June 29th. These storms moved across central Minnesota, between Alexandria, Little Falls, and Mora, and produced severe wind gusts, with isolated tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power line was blown down north of Little Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of June 9th. These storms produced locally heavy rains with the runoff resulting in flash flooding across Fillmore County. Several rural roads were washed out or underwater near Harmony across the western and southern sections of the county including State Highway 16 east of Spring Valley. A deputy sheriff was stranded by flood waters near Granger before moving to higher ground and finding an exit. The highest reported rainfall total was 6 inches in Spring Valley. The Fillmore County Board of Supervisors declared a state of emergency with estimated damages of over $200,000 in Bristol and Forestville Townships.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous rural roads were washed out or under water across the western and southern portions of Fillmore County and near Harmony. This included State Highway 16 east of Spring Valley. County Road 8 was washed out about five miles east of Spring Valley. A deputy sheriff was stranded by flood waters between County Road 30 and Klondike Road near Granger before moving to higher ground and finding an exit. Bristol and Forestville Townships suffered the most extensive damages with estimates of over $200,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms that developed across west central Minnesota Sunday morning, June 17th, moved quickly northeast across central Minnesota by the early afternoon. The line of storms became severe east of Long Prairie and produced a concentrated area of intense damage. Hundreds of trees were blown down, including a barn that was totally destroyed. It also produced damage near Cushing and Lincoln, but it wasn't as severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on a power line on U.S. Highway 10 near Lincoln.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large complex of thunderstorms, developed across North Dakota Tuesday evening, June 5th, and moved east-southeast into western Minnesota after midnight. The complex raced east-southeast across central, west central, and southwest Minnesota during the early morning hours. The Swift county airport did report a severe wind gust of 58 knots. However, most reporting sites had wind gusts between 30 and 45 mph as the line of storms moved across Minnesota. After 6 AM CST, the line of storms weakened rapidly with only heavy rainfall and some small hail reported after sunrise.

It also produced damage near Cushing and Lincoln, but it wasn't as severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A farm was severely damaged as a concentrated area of wind damage occurred northeast of Long Prairie. The farm was located on Indigo Drive where the owner reported hundreds of trees blown down on his farm. The barn was completely destroyed with damage to a fence and home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two lines of severe thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of June 30th. These storms dropped some hail and also produced damaging winds. Quarter sized hail fell near Preston (Fillmore County) and Winona (Winona County). A 69 mph wind gust occurred at the Rochester airport (Olmsted County) and trees and power lines were blown down in Austin (Mower County) and Lanesboro (Fillmore County). A lightning strike started a house fire in Goodview (Winona County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Austin.

The line of storms featured a bow echo leading to numerous wind damage reports. Lightning also struck a cabin injuring a person sleeping inside the cabin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large oak trees were blown down on Olson Street. Many trees were blown down around Pequot Lakes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a cabin just north of the Brainerd International Raceway. It went through the roof and struck a 15 year-old boy who was lying in bed before passing through the boxspring of the bed and exiting the cabin through an electrical outlet. The boy received  superficial injuries. The bed he was on started on fire and there was damage to the electrical system of the cabin and an air conditioner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of storms moved from South Dakota, eastward into southern Minnesota the morning of Saturday, June 9th. Rainfall rates were nearly 2 inches per hour as thunderstorms began to train along the Minnesota River around Belle Plaine and Carver. A few local private rain gauges and radar estimates reported nearly 3 to 4 inches of rain within two hours around Carver. A Twitter picture was sent that provided details of debris flowing over the road near Jonathan Carver Parkway, and Main Street in Carver, due to the excessive rainfall. By the late morning, thunderstorms developed in Freeborn County and produced golf ball sized hail west of Clarks Grove.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of large hail, up to golf ball size, occurred northeast of Manchester, and west of Clarks Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms that developed across the Dakotas late Sunday night, moved into western Minnesota Monday morning. Several thunderstorms continued to redevelop from earlier outflow boundaries. These storms moved across eastern Minnesota, and dissipated as much drier air was moving westward into the same area. As the thunderstorms decayed, strong winds developed and produced strong, to locally severe winds for a period of 30 mins to one hour. The strongest winds occurred around Lake Minnetonka where some boats were capsized and damaged occurred on docks and along the shore line. Most of the measured wind gusts were between 45 and 55 mph across Stearns, Benton, Wright and Sherburne Counties. There was even a report of crop damage around Cokota in Wright County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The St. Cloud Regional airport measured a wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong thunderstorms developed in southern Minnesota and drifted to the north and produced strong winds of 40 to 50 mph. There were a few measured wind gusts in the morning which included St. James airport with a 48 mph wind gust, Fairmont with a 40 mph wind gust, Mankato with a 41 mph wind gust, Owatonna with a 45 mph wind gust, and the St. Paul downtown airport with a 46 mph wind gust. Other storms developed in west central Minnesota prior to sunrise, and produced nickel size hail near Carlos.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local park ranger reported nickel size hail for approximately 3 minutes northwest of Carlos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms moved across portions of southeast Minnesota during the evening of July 12th. These storms blew down some trees and power lines near Elgin (Wabasha County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down near Elgin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As scattered showers formed over southeast Minnesota during the late morning and afternoon of July 29th, funnel clouds were sighted near Utica and Lewiston (Winona County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted over Utica.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two lines of severe thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of June 30th. These storms dropped some hail and also produced damaging winds. Quarter sized hail fell near Preston (Fillmore County) and Winona (Winona County). A 69 mph wind gust occurred at the Rochester airport (Olmsted County) and trees and power lines were blown down in Austin (Mower County) and Lanesboro (Fillmore County). A lightning strike started a house fire in Goodview (Winona County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on a farm north of Grand Meadow. One of the trees landed on the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was located across central Minnesota into southwest Wisconsin. Low level flow maintained the influx of moisture and warm air as a shortwave topped a ridge in place to the west. This led to storms throughout much of the day, a few of which were severe with damaging winds and hail. The storms also contained heavy rainfall which primed parts of the area for major flash flooding the following night.

Southeast Minnesota was covered with 6 to 9 inches of snow on April 3rd as an early spring snow storm moved across the region.  Impacts from the snow were rather limited as it mainly fell during the daytime allowing melting to occur on most roads. Some schools did close for the day on the 3rd with several having late starts on the 4th. The highest reported total was 9 inches near La Crescent (Houston County), near Wabasha (Wabasha County) and in Plainview (Wabasha County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 6 to 8 inches of snow fell across Dodge County. The highest reported total was 8.7 inches near Kasson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third and final day of a three day severe weather event across the Northland. A stalled out frontal system finally made a push across the area leading to another round of severe storms and heavy rainfall over areas that had seen dramatic flash flooding the previous night. Widespread rainfall amounts of 1-2 inches plus over northeastern Minnesota and northwestern Wisconsin sent already swollen creeks and rivers out of their banks and over roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Highway 65 from Libby to McGregor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By the early evening of July 8th, a frontal boundary was draped from International Falls to southwest of Jamestown. South of this boundary, temperatures were in the mid to upper 80s with dew points in the middle 70s. Thunderstorms initially fired over Becker County, Minnesota, but other storms soon moved into southeast North Dakota as well. The storms over southeast North Dakota tracked into west central Minnesota by mid evening. These storms produced both damaging winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage was reported throughout town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: River levels already high from excessive rains during late June received another push upward from heavy rainfall on July 1-3. Rainfall from one to two inches was common upstream in the basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant amounts of crop and pasture land near the Rock River were impacted by minor flooding as river levels climbed to 2.7 feet above flood stage near Hardwick (gage site HRDM5) on July 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several periods of snowfall wrapped into west central Minnesota around a very large upper level low pressure system from the 13th to the 15th.  Heavy snowfall of 9 to 13 inches combined with winds up to 40 mph at times to produce near blizzard conditions. Several schools and businesses were closed across the county on the 13th. Many events were also postponed or cancelled. Travel was difficult, if not impossible for much of the period. Snowfall amounts include 9 inches at Artichoke Lake and 13 inches at Ortonville.

On the morning of July 19th, widespread cloud cover and steady showers were ongoing across southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota. Meanwhile, further northwest, fairly abundant late morning and afternoon sunshine occurred over the Devils Lake region. By late afternoon and early evening, a weak surface boundary moved into the Devils Lake region and helped to set off scattered strong to severe thunderstorms. Atmospheric conditions were also conducive for funnel clouds, and several of these were also reported.

A cold front moved into the northwest quarter of Minnesota during the evening of the 11th into the early morning hours of the 12th. Ahead of this cold front, temperatures over the northwest quarter of Minnesota during the early evening of the 11th ranged well into the 80s, with dew points in the middle 70s, so it was very warm and muggy. Thunderstorms broke out along the front, producing funnel clouds, tornadoes, large hail, and damaging wind gusts.

During the early morning hours of July 4th, surface low pressure had set up over north central South Dakota, with an east-to-west warm front extending eastward from the low along the North Dakota/South Dakota border into west central Minnesota. Temperatures across eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota ranged in the 70s with dew points in the upper 60s to low 70s. The mid level winds (500mb) were from the south-southwest. A bowing line of thunderstorms, stretching from south of Rugby to the North Dakota/South Dakota border moved into portions of the southwest Devils Lake region just after 130 am CDT. Following the mid level wind flow, the storms tracked to the east-northeast, reaching the Red River Valley around 4 am CDT. At this point, the bowing line of storms stretched from southern Manitoba down into northeast South Dakota. This bowing line of storms tracked through the northwest quarter of Minnesota in the 4 to 6 am CDT time frame, so these storms were moving quite quickly. Most of the storms had exited the Baudette to Park Rapids corridor by 7 am CDT. These storms produced widespread damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was snapped in a farm yard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the terminus of a 35 to 40 knot low-level jet, significant moisture transport and convergence contributed to several hours of slow-moving thunderstorms which traversed across the same locations.  Rainfall from 4 to 8 inches was common in much of Lyon, Murray and Cottonwood counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several gravel township and county roads were under water near Holland, Ruthton and Woodstock.

This bowing line of storms tracked through the northwest quarter of Minnesota in the 4 to 6 am CDT time frame, so these storms were moving quite quickly. Most of the storms had exited the Baudette to Park Rapids corridor by 7 am CDT. These storms produced widespread damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage was reported.

Temperatures across eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota ranged in the 70s with dew points in the upper 60s to low 70s. The mid level winds (500mb) were from the south-southwest. A bowing line of thunderstorms, stretching from south of Rugby to the North Dakota/South Dakota border moved into portions of the southwest Devils Lake region just after 130 am CDT. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold temperatures aloft and daytime heating helped contribute to hit and miss afternoon and early evening showers and mainly weak thunderstorms. However, one thunderstorm over Polk County, Minnesota, did become severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in the afternoon of July 31st, a cold front extended from International Falls (MN) to Fargo (ND). Just after 4 pm CDT, thunderstorms formed along the front over portions of Clearwater and Beltrami counties, and dropped some large hail. By 7 pm CDT, thunderstorms formed even further westward along the front, or back into the Fargo-Moorhead area. This first line moved southeast and gradually weakened. Then, another line of thunderstorms formed after 9 pm CDT from Bemidji to the Fargo-Moorhead area. As this line moved southeast, it also dropped some large hail.

Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of Wednesday, July 25th, across southern, and east central Minnesota. These storms quickly became severe in south central Minnesota, where there was a swath of large hail from Sanborn, southeast to east of Comfrey, then from Darfur, to Truman, Huntley and Blue Earth. The largest hail stone was reported near Blue Earth with a measurement of 2.75 in diameter. Other storms produced hail in east central Minnesota, but reports were less than quarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the terminus of a 35 to 40 knot low-level jet, significant moisture transport and convergence contributed to several hours of slow-moving thunderstorms which traversed across the same locations.  Rainfall from 4 to 8 inches was common in much of Lyon, Murray and Cottonwood counties.

A band of elevated thunderstorms progressed across the area during the morning. While deep-layer shear was minimal, the low-level shear and high degree of DCAPE suggested short term organization to storms given decent elevated instability.

An early Spring storm moved out of the Rockies, swinging across Nebraska early on the morning of the 24th. Precipitation blossomed within the leading warm advection region, including a few thunderstorms on the evening of the 23rd, which transitioned from rain to snow lifting northeast into southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall, starting as a brief period of rain, became moderate to briefly heavy by late evening on March 23 and into the early morning hours of March 24. Visibility dropped to less than a mile at times, with snowfall accumulating to 2 to 5 inches, including 5.0 inches at Worthington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the northwest quarter of Minnesota during the evening of the 11th into the early morning hours of the 12th. Ahead of this cold front, temperatures over the northwest quarter of Minnesota during the early evening of the 11th ranged well into the 80s, with dew points in the middle 70s, so it was very warm and muggy. Thunderstorms broke out along the front, producing funnel clouds, tornadoes, large hail, and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop and tree damage was reported. Trees estimated to be about a foot in diameter were snapped or uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms erupted along an earlier outflow boundary by the middle of the afternoon. MLCAPE was extensive ahead of the boundary around 3000 J/kg, but shear was fairly weak favoring multicell structure.  With mid-level dry air and steep low-level lapse rates, the severe mode suggested was damaging wind.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple funnel cloud reports were received from the Lake Melissa area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from 4 to 6 inches across the Redwood River basin near Marshall resulted in a rapid rise above flood stage by 0500CST, with a record crest at gage site MSHM5 of 17.41 feet at 0930CST.  Numerous water rescues of stranded motorists were required around Marshall.  Intermittent power outages occurred around Marshall, impacting the water and waste water treatment plants.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of snow melt and rainfall brought minor flooding to portions of Split Rock Creek below Split Rock Lake.  Cresting at minor flood stage occurred below Jasper (JSPM5), impacting only lowland agricultural areas and a couple of low water crossings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall along with snow melt caused minor flooding of lowland agricultural areas along Split Rock Creek near Jasper. The river reached a crest of 1.9 feet above flood stage below Jasper (gage site JSPM5) on March 6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado moved across rural portions of far northeast Fillmore County into southwest Winona County. The tornado started northwest of Rushford (Fillmore County) and was on the ground for about 5 minutes causing minor damage to a house and trees. Several rounds of thunderstorms moved across the area dumping heavy rain. The heavy caused street flooding to occur in Rochester (Olmsted County) with cars becoming trapped in the flood waters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused street flooding to occur in Hayfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense negative tilt upper-level trough pushed out across the Rockies and into the Plains on March 5, deepening into a very strong closed low across Iowa by early on March 6. 500 hPa heights fell as much as 27 dam in 24 hours as the system deepened. As a result, winds increased to a gusty 30 to 40 mph and produced brief periods of near blizzard conditions. The system was dynamic enough to produce a few thunderstorms early on March 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hazardous winter weather evolved during the morning on March 5, as rain changed to light to moderate snowfall and was accompanied by winds gusting as high as 35 mph.  Total snowfall was generally 3 to 6 inches, including 4.2 inches at Windom, 4.1 inches 2 miles northeast of Lakefield, 4.0 inches at Worthington, 3.5 inches at Lake Wilson and 3.0 inches at Pipestone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a larger scale bowing segment in eastern South Dakota, an isolated thunderstorm developed across southwest Minnesota on the nose of a strong low-level jet.  The storm pulsed briefly stronger and produced hail across areas near and in the city of Marshall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickel size hail fell, but spotter was not under the stronger part of the storm.

During the mid evening of September 14th, a thunderstorm flared up east of Jamestown and tracked to the east. This storm moved along the Interstate 94 corridor, through the Valley City and Tower City areas, eventually passing north of the Fargo-Moorhead area. As this large storm tracked east-northeast, other thunderstorms also strengthened along a line from Twin Valley to Mahnomen to Bemidji (in Minnesota). These storms mainly produced large hail, but there were also several reports of damaging winds.

An intense negative tilt upper-level trough pushed out across the Rockies and into the Plains on March 5, deepening into a very strong closed low across Iowa by early on March 6. 500 hPa heights fell as much as 27 dam in 24 hours as the system deepened. As a result, winds increased to a gusty 30 to 40 mph and produced brief periods of near blizzard conditions. The system was dynamic enough to produce a few thunderstorms early on March 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hazardous winter weather evolved during the morning on March 5, as rain changed to light to moderate snowfall and was accompanied by winds gusting as high as 35 mph.  Total snowfall was generally 3 to 6 inches, including 4.2 inches at Windom, 4.1 inches 2 miles northeast of Lakefield, 4.0 inches at Worthington, 3.5 inches at Lake Wilson and 3.0 inches at Pipestone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in the afternoon of July 31st, a cold front extended from International Falls (MN) to Fargo (ND). Just after 4 pm CDT, thunderstorms formed along the front over portions of Clearwater and Beltrami counties, and dropped some large hail. By 7 pm CDT, thunderstorms formed even further westward along the front, or back into the Fargo-Moorhead area. This first line moved southeast and gradually weakened. Then, another line of thunderstorms formed after 9 pm CDT from Bemidji to the Fargo-Moorhead area. As this line moved southeast, it also dropped some large hail.

Just after 430 pm CDT, thunderstorms formed along the front, midway between Devils Lake and Grand Forks. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few strong thunderstorms became severe over northwest Minnesota during the mid to late afternoon of September 12th. As the Minnesota storms approached the Roseau to Baudette area, another area of storms initiated over the Devils Lake region. These North Dakota storms eventually tracked up into the northern Red River Valley. Both areas of storms produced hail.

During the morning of March 10, an approaching weak mid-level wave brought a brief pulse of warm advection.  With cold temperatures at the surface and warmer temperatures aloft, a mix of precipitation types occurred.  Most freezing rain was brief and light across southwest Minnesota, with snow the predominant type.  While most snowfall was less than an inch, a narrow band of one to four inches occurred from Pipestone to Worthington along the Buffalo Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A persistent light snowfall started with a brief mix of light freezing rain, resulting in a light glaze of icing.  Total snowfall at Pipestone through the evening on March 10 was 3.5 inches, with 2.0 inches at Edgerton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain produced less than a tenth of an inch ice accumulation, glazing untreated surfaces with a thin coating of ice.  A brief period of light snowfall as precipitation ended produced less than an inch of snow.

With river levels already running high due to late June rainfall, additional heavy rainfall of four to nine inches the first few days of July brought increased flooding to the Little Sioux River and tributaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant amounts of crop and pasture land were flooded as river levels on the Little Sioux River climbed to 1.7 feet above flood stage near Montgomery (gage site MLSI4) on July 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With river levels already running high due to late June rainfall, additional heavy rainfall of five to nine inches the first few days of July brought increased flooding to the West Fork of the Des Moines River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 3 to 5 inches (locally 5 to 10 inches upstream) during the first three days of July exacerbated flooding through the Des Moines River basin. A state of emergency was declared in the City of Windom with water breaching Island Park, the golf course, and several homes. Several county, state and local roadways flooded for up to a week, including highway 62 and numerous city streets. Significant amounts of crop and pasture land near the Des Moines River were severely impacted by major flooding as river levels climbed to the second highest crest on record at 4.4 feet above flood stage at Windom (gage site WDOM5) on July 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding persisted through the night and early morning hours across much of Southwest Minnesota in Lyon, Murray and northwest portions of Cottonwood county after heavy rainfall from the prior early morning.  Impacts continued to feature homes getting water into the lower levels and flooded roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S Highway 59 remained closed around 191st Street with significant water over the roadway. Numerous basements in Currie and Slayton were flooded and city streets remained impassable due to standing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado moved across rural portions of far northeast Fillmore County into southwest Winona County. The tornado started northwest of Rushford (Fillmore County) and was on the ground for about 5 minutes causing minor damage to a house and trees. Several rounds of thunderstorms moved across the area dumping heavy rain. The heavy caused street flooding to occur in Rochester (Olmsted County) with cars becoming trapped in the flood waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near Waltham, 4.56 inches of rain fell.

At the terminus of a 35 to 40 knot low-level jet, significant moisture transport and convergence contributed to several hours of slow-moving thunderstorms which traversed across the same locations.  Rainfall from 4 to 8 inches was common in much of Lyon, Murray and Cottonwood counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported flowing over County Road 7 near the intersection with State Highway 19 in western portions of Marshall. Rainfall at Marshall was 4.81 inches, with 4.02 inches just south of Marshall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The large hail was accompanied by strong winds and very heavy rain. The winds tracked across the north end of Lake Lida.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed late Thursday morning across south central Minnesota. These storms developed along a very apparent west to east wind shift line. Several funnel clouds were reported along this line. One of the stronger storms had enough strength to develop a weak tornado west of Ceylon, Minnesota. The tornado was recorded on video from a local sheriff as it slowly moved across an open farm field. Most of the damage was associated with a row of trees before it dissipated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local police department reported funnel clouds west of Benson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: River levels already high from excessive rains during late June received another push upward from heavy rainfall on July 1-3. Rainfall from one to two inches was common upstream in the basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant amounts of crop and pasture land near the Rock River were impacted by minor flooding as river levels climbed to 2.7 feet above flood stage near Hardwick (gage site HRDM5) on July 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the terminus of a 35 to 40 knot low-level jet, significant moisture transport and convergence contributed to several hours of slow-moving thunderstorms which traversed across the same locations.  Rainfall from 4 to 8 inches was common in much of Lyon, Murray and Cottonwood counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive rainfall rates overloaded culverts on County Road 5 north of Lynd and County Road 7 south of State Highway 23 near Marshall, each washing out large sections of roadway.  Other county roads (60, 63 and 65) lost culverts as well, with road damage across the county totaling around 155 thousand dollars. Rainfall at Marshall was 4.81 inches, 4.02 inches just south of Marshall, 5.77 inches at Tracy and 6.50 inches at Ghent.

Rainfall from 4 to 8 inches was common in much of Lyon, Murray and Cottonwood counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant water was reported over U.S. Highway 14 in many areas between Tracy and Walnut Grove, with the road closed at this time by the Minnesota Dept. of Transportation. 5.77 inches of rainfall was reported at Tracy in just a couple of hours.

As the Minnesota storms approached the Roseau to Baudette area, another area of storms initiated over the Devils Lake region. These North Dakota storms eventually tracked up into the northern Red River Valley. Both areas of storms produced hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nocturnal thunderstorms developed in a narrow band between 0100 and 0500CST across Rock and eastern Minnehaha counties. The storms produced from 1 to 2 inches of rain in a two hour period, which fell on fully frozen ground. Runoff from rainfall and snowmelt caused significant flow increases in Beaver Creek from north of Hardwick, MN to south of Brandon, SD.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain and snow melt caused inundation of low-lying stretches of township roads near 50th Avenue and 91st Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado moved across rural portions of far northeast Fillmore County into southwest Winona County. The tornado started northwest of Rushford (Fillmore County) and was on the ground for about 5 minutes causing minor damage to a house and trees. Several rounds of thunderstorms moved across the area dumping heavy rain. The heavy caused street flooding to occur in Rochester (Olmsted County) with cars becoming trapped in the flood waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Mantorville, 4.17 inches of rain fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With river levels already running high due to late June rainfall, additional heavy rainfall of three to eight inches the first few days of July brought renewed flooding to the Redwood River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant amounts of crop and pasture land near the Redwood River were severely impacted by moderate flooding as river levels climbed to 4.2 feet above flood stage at Russell (gage site RUSM5) on July 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With river levels already running high due to late June rainfall, additional heavy rainfall of three to eight inches the first few days of July brought renewed flooding to the Redwood River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The event began as a flash flood, with historical flooding of areas around Marshall. Numerous motorists were in need of rescue when their vehicles became stalled in the flood waters which ran 2 to 4 feet deep in places around the city.  Significant amounts of crop and pasture land were severely impacted near the Redwood River, as well as a few county and township roads inundated by major flooding as river levels climbed to 3.4 feet above flood stage near Marshall (gage site MSHM5) on July 3. This is the highest crest on record for the gage site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With river levels already running high due to late June rainfall, additional heavy rainfall of five to nine inches the first few days of July brought increased flooding to the West Fork of the Des Moines River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous county and township roads, including county highway 6 and 44 were inundated for almost a week.  Significant amounts of crop and pasture land near the Des Moines River were severely impacted by major flooding as river levels climbed to 5.2 feet above flood stage near Avoca (gage site AVOM5) on July 5.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant water reported over roads and into yards. Report received via social media.  Rainfall of 5.77 inches was reported in Tracy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early Spring storm moved out of the Rockies, swinging across Nebraska early on the morning of the 24th. Precipitation blossomed within the leading warm advection region, including a few thunderstorms on the evening of the 23rd, which transitioned from rain to snow lifting northeast into southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall, starting as a brief period of rain, became moderate to briefly heavy by late evening on March 23 and into the early morning hours of March 24. Visibility dropped to less than a mile at times, with snowfall accumulating to 2 to 5 inches, including 5.0 inches at Worthington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: River levels already high from excessive rains during late June received another push upward from heavy rainfall on July 1. Rainfall from one to two inches was common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop and pasture land was inundated as levels on Flandreau Creek climbed to 1.8 feet above flood stage above Flandreau (gage site FCRS2) on July 1. After dropping briefly below flood stage, the river returned above flood stage with moderate flooding 2.1 feet above flood stage on July 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With river levels already running high due to late June rainfall, additional heavy rainfall of five to nine inches the first few days of July brought increased flooding to the West Fork of the Des Moines River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A state of emergency was declared as rainfall from 3 to 5 inches (locally 5 to 10 inches upstream) during the first three days of July exacerbated flooding through the Des Moines River basin. Several county, state and local roadways flooded for several days and the city parks in Jackson were inundated.  Significant amounts of crop and pasture land near the Des Moines River were severely impacted by moderate flooding as river levels climbed to 1.9 feet above flood stage at Jackson (gage site JCKM5) on July 8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With river levels already running high due to late June rainfall, additional heavy rainfall of five to nine inches the first few days of July brought increased flooding to the West Fork of the Des Moines River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 3 to 5 inches (locally 5 to 10 inches upstream) during the first three days of July exacerbated flooding through the Des Moines River basin.  A state of emergency was declared in the City of Windom with water breaching Island Park, the golf course, and several homes. Several county, state and local roadways flooded for up to a week, including highway 62 and numerous city streets.  Significant amounts of crop and pasture land near the Des Moines River were severely impacted by major flooding as river levels climbed to the second highest crest on record at 4.4 feet above flood stage at Windom (gage site WDOM5) on July 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the terminus of a 35 to 40 knot low-level jet, significant moisture transport and convergence contributed to several hours of slow-moving thunderstorms which traversed across the same locations.  Rainfall from 4 to 8 inches was common in much of Lyon, Murray and Cottonwood counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall produced widespread flooding of basements around Lake Shetek as water levels of the lake rapidly rose above shore.  8.61 inches of rain was measured at Lake Shetek in just a few hours with over 7 inches in just a two hour period.  Around 70 children were evacuated from a Bible camp as water inundated the campground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm developed along a frontal boundary during the late afternoon in an environment with around 2000 J/kg MLCAPE and deep-layer shear of 40-50 knots.  Rotating storms developed with hail, with strong winds also supported by around 1000 J/kg DCAPE.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds contributed to a period of wind-driven hail from dime to quarter size.  Soybeans were stripped to stubble and leaves ripped off trees.  A damage path varied from one to two miles in width where crop losses were nearly total.  The duration of the event was around 8 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm developed along a frontal boundary during the late afternoon in an environment with around 2000 J/kg MLCAPE and deep-layer shear of 40-50 knots.  Rotating storms developed with hail, with strong winds also supported by around 1000 J/kg DCAPE.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quickly-spreading grass fire burned over 1000 acres and destroyed a barn north of the town of Nashwauk. There were no injuries and the fire was put out in about an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two individuals were killed with three others injured, one with life-threatening injuries, when one vehicle skidded into another. Pavement was wet from recent rainfall. According to the MN State Patrol, all involved in the crash were wearing seat belts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two individuals were killed with three others injured, one with life-threatening injuries, when one vehicle skidded into another. Pavement was wet from recent rainfall. According to the MN State Patrol, all involved in the crash were wearing seat belts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and unstable airmass was in place ahead of a shortwave and low-level convergence moving into the area. This lead to storms during the afternoon and evening hours that produced numerous reports of downed trees and power lines due to strong winds along with small hail with the heaviest hit areas stretching from the Brainerd Lakes area to the Iron Range. Power outages from this event lasted for over a day in some areas following the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 14 inch diameter maple tree was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front moved across the Northland led to a few thunderstorms with marginally severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported 3 minutes of dime-sized hail at Gooseberry Falls State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms that developed in northern Minnesota, sagged southward into central Minnesota during the morning of Thursday, July 12th. High rainfall rates, and training of the storms, caused up to five inches of rain (based on radar) in less than three hours in northern Kanabec County. This amount of rainfall produced flash flooding, and local officials indicated damage to many county roads throughout the county. The heaviest rainfall total was 7.04 inches near Mora. One of the storms moved quickly across southern Mille Lacs County and produced wind damage in Princeton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tin roof blew off a business in Princeton.  The roof came off in three large pieces and landed in an adjacent parking lot.  It appears to have been tin sheets placed over the shingles.  The shingle roof was still in place.  The tin landed on an SUV in the parking lot and caused damage to the vehicle.  A few power poles, and power lines were also blown down in Princeton.

Power outages from this event lasted for over a day in some areas following the storms.

Around sunrise on May 27th, a line of thunderstorms was located from Cooperstown to Jamestown. This line held together as it moved southeast, eventually passing through the Wahpeton-Breckenridge and Fergus Falls areas. There were isolated reports of strong winds as the storms moved through.
A strong thunderstorm over Hubbard County caused some damage west-southwest of Lake George.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few small poplar trees and some larger branches were blown down near the intersection of highways 113 and 71. A few minutes of dime sized hail also fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: May 24th was a very warm day, with temperatures peaking in the mid 80s to around 90 degrees. It was also a fairly humid day for May, with dew points in the upper 50s to lower 60s. Scattered thunderstorms broke out over the Red River Valley during early evening, eventually congealing into a more continuous line over northwest Minnesota by late evening. These storms produced large hail and strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail dime sized or larger covered the ground.

Scattered thunderstorms broke out over the Red River Valley during early evening, eventually congealing into a more continuous line over northwest Minnesota by late evening. These storms produced large hail and strong wind gusts.

Weak, unorganized surface low pressure was located over the North Dakota/Minnesota region on the afternoon of May 29th. However, temperatures were quite mild, generally in the mid to upper 80s with dew points in the 60s. Thunderstorms over west central Minnesota in the early afternoon tracked north-northeast through the late afternoon and early evening, mainly affecting the Wadena to Bemidji to Baudette corridor. Some of these storms dropped large hail and produced strong wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms that developed in northern Minnesota, sagged southward into central Minnesota during the morning of Thursday, July 12th. High rainfall rates, and training of the storms, caused up to five inches of rain (based on radar) in less than three hours in northern Kanabec County. This amount of rainfall produced flash flooding, and local officials indicated damage to many county roads throughout the county. The heaviest rainfall total was 7.04 inches near Mora. One of the storms moved quickly across southern Mille Lacs County and produced wind damage in Princeton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed around Mora due to high running water and stranded cars. A few inches of flowing water covered Highway 3, near the intersection of Highway 65, north of Mora.  Plus, the county sheriff officials reported that nearly all gravel roads in Kanabec County suffered damage from flood waters. The Fish Lake Resort that is on south side of the lake was flood damage due to the rising lake level. Trailers in the resort had flood damage as water levels impacted the park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of Wednesday, July 25th, across southern, and east central Minnesota. These storms quickly became severe in south central Minnesota, where there was a swath of large hail from Sanborn, southeast to east of Comfrey, then from Darfur, to Truman, Huntley and Blue Earth. The largest hail stone was reported near Blue Earth with a measurement of 2.75 in diameter. Other storms produced hail in east central Minnesota, but reports were less than quarters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of very large hail, up to baseball size, occurred from south of Huntley, southeast to south-southeast of Blue Earth. Crops were reported shredded in this area due to the extremely large hail and wind. The COOP observer in Blue Earth reported up to quarter size hail during this time period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple rounds of thunderstorms swept across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of May 25th. These storms primarily produced large hail with some occasional damaging winds. The largest reported hail size was quarter sized in the Whitewater State Park (Winona County) and Lewiston (Winona County). Trees and power lines were blown down between Houston and Brownsville (Houston County) and across the northern part of Dodge County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and some power lines were blown down near Brownsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several complexes of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of Thursday, May 24th, and produced several reports of down trees, power lines, and a few large hail stones. Four areas had the most damage, one was in the west side of the Twin Cities, and another near the downtown area. Another area was from Faribault, northeast to Cannon Falls. One other area was south and east of Alexandria. All of the activity quickly died off once the sun set. There were several wind sensors that measured wind gusts between 50 to 55 mph, or just below severe wind limits in the western part of the Twin Cities Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported blown down west of Osseo.

The afternoon of Tuesday, May 29th, thunderstorms began to develop across southwest Minnesota, and move northward. Most of the storms were sub-severe with occasional gusts of 40 to 50 mph noted on airport ASOS/AWOS sites. Other thunderstorms began to develop across south central Minnesota and moved northward toward the Twin Cities area. ||By the mid to late afternoon, temperatures rose into the 90s with CAPE values of 1500-2000 J/Kg. Due to weak wind shear, most of the storms were outflow dominant, but occasional severe wind gusts occurred with the stronger cores. Two storms produced significant wind damage near Geneva, north of Albert Lea, Minnesota, and Hutchinson, Minnesota. A turkey barn was destroyed near Geneva, and three people were injured but recovered from their injuries. Around Hutchinson, there were numerous reports of downed trees and power lines. Some of the trees caused damage to homes.  The local emergency manager in McLeod County determined that there were about 5-6 houses in Hutchinson that received structural damage from trees.||In addition, McLeod County declared a state of emergency on May 30, 2018 as a result of these high winds. An initial damage assessment was conducted and determined that there was about $54,000 in estimated public assistance damages.||Other storms produced an occasional hail stone up to quarter size. However, most of the storms were only producing dime to nickel size hail with wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report was received via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level wave pushed across the northern Plains on May 8, with a frontal zone moving in during the evening hours. Even with modest instability with MLCAPE from 500-1000 J/kg, a few storms produced near severe hail.

A very moist and unstable airmass was in place ahead of a shortwave and low-level convergence moving into the area. This lead to storms during the afternoon and evening hours that produced numerous reports of downed trees and power lines due to strong winds along with small hail with the heaviest hit areas stretching from the Brainerd Lakes area to the Iron Range. Power outages from this event lasted for over a day in some areas following the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel-size hail was reported on Wilkins Lake.

This amount of rainfall produced flash flooding, and local officials indicated damage to many county roads throughout the county. The heaviest rainfall total was 7.04 inches near Mora. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms developed Friday afternoon along a cold front, amidst a moisture rich air mass. Another area of storms developed across the eastern Dakotas, and raced eastward across central, and southern Minnesota. The storms along the front produced a weak landspout tornado near Minnesota Lake. Other storms produced marginal severe hail in the northwest part of the Twin Cities, with larger hail, up to golf ball size, west of Albert Lea.

The afternoon of Tuesday, May 29th, thunderstorms began to develop across southwest Minnesota, and move northward. Most of the storms were sub-severe with occasional gusts of 40 to 50 mph noted on airport ASOS/AWOS sites. Other thunderstorms began to develop across south central Minnesota and moved northward toward the Twin Cities area. ||By the mid to late afternoon, temperatures rose into the 90s with CAPE values of 1500-2000 J/Kg. Due to weak wind shear, most of the storms were outflow dominant, but occasional severe wind gusts occurred with the stronger cores. Two storms produced significant wind damage near Geneva, north of Albert Lea, Minnesota, and Hutchinson, Minnesota. A turkey barn was destroyed near Geneva, and three people were injured but recovered from their injuries. Around Hutchinson, there were numerous reports of downed trees and power lines. Some of the trees caused damage to homes.  The local emergency manager in McLeod County determined that there were about 5-6 houses in Hutchinson that received structural damage from trees.||In addition, McLeod County declared a state of emergency on May 30, 2018 as a result of these high winds. An initial damage assessment was conducted and determined that there was about $54,000 in estimated public assistance damages.||Other storms produced an occasional hail stone up to quarter size. However, most of the storms were only producing dime to nickel size hail with wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Water from snow melt and rainfall continued to work through the Big Sioux River early in May. Flooding remained generally minor with impacts limited to agricultural areas near the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow fall through minor flood stages persisted along the Des Moines River during much of the first week of May, with impacts generally limited to flooding of agricultural areas near the river. Flooding ended near Windom (gage site WDOM5) on May 5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple rounds of thunderstorms swept across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of May 25th. These storms primarily produced large hail with some occasional damaging winds. The largest reported hail size was quarter sized in the Whitewater State Park (Winona County) and Lewiston (Winona County). Trees and power lines were blown down between Houston and Brownsville (Houston County) and across the northern part of Dodge County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell in the Whitewater State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southern Minnesota had quite the day with record heat and afternoon and early evening thunderstorms. First, several areas across southern Minnesota, including the Twin Cities International Airport, reported record high temperatures for Memorial Day. 100 Degrees was the highest and earliest recorded 100 degree temperature at the Twin Cities airport since records were started over 150 years ago. Other areas reported 100 degrees in southern Minnesota, including Mankato. ||The heat and humidity was the source of an unstable atmosphere across Minnesota. An earlier outflow boundary from thunderstorms in Wisconsin, moved westward and ignited a broken line of thunderstorms in northern Iowa, and southern Minnesota, south of Mankato, prior to 2 pm. These storms produced an outflow boundary and raced northward toward the Twin Cities metro area. Thunderstorms continued to form along the outflow boundary and a few caused severe hail, and damaging wind gusts. The largest hail stone was reported near Avon, where 2 inch diameter hail was observed and recorded on video. Elsewhere, thunderstorms that moved across the northern metro area, developed an intense downburst wind, and caused an area of large trees that were blown down between Blaine, Ham Lake, Columbus, and Lino Lakes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple rounds of thunderstorms swept across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of May 25th. These storms primarily produced large hail with some occasional damaging winds. The largest reported hail size was quarter sized in the Whitewater State Park (Winona County) and Lewiston (Winona County). Trees and power lines were blown down between Houston and Brownsville (Houston County) and across the northern part of Dodge County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport measured a wind gust of 60 mph.
