EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon of May 29th across southeast Minnesota. These storms dropped large hail across Dodge and Olmsted Counties. The largest hail fell in the vicinity of Rock Dell (Olmsted County) where golf ball to two inch diameter hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to two inch hail was reported just north of Rock Dell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several complexes of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of Thursday, May 24th, and produced several reports of down trees, power lines, and a few large hail stones. Four areas had the most damage, one was in the west side of the Twin Cities, and another near the downtown area. Another area was from Faribault, northeast to Cannon Falls. One other area was south and east of Alexandria. All of the activity quickly died off once the sun set. There were several wind sensors that measured wind gusts between 50 to 55 mph, or just below severe wind limits in the western part of the Twin Cities Metro area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southern Minnesota had quite the day with record heat and afternoon and early evening thunderstorms. First, several areas across southern Minnesota, including the Twin Cities International Airport, reported record high temperatures for Memorial Day. 100 Degrees was the highest and earliest recorded 100 degree temperature at the Twin Cities airport since records were started over 150 years ago. Other areas reported 100 degrees in southern Minnesota, including Mankato. ||The heat and humidity was the source of an unstable atmosphere across Minnesota. An earlier outflow boundary from thunderstorms in Wisconsin, moved westward and ignited a broken line of thunderstorms in northern Iowa, and southern Minnesota, south of Mankato, prior to 2 pm. These storms produced an outflow boundary and raced northward toward the Twin Cities metro area. Thunderstorms continued to form along the outflow boundary and a few caused severe hail, and damaging wind gusts. The largest hail stone was reported near Avon, where 2 inch diameter hail was observed and recorded on video. Elsewhere, thunderstorms that moved across the northern metro area, developed an intense downburst wind, and caused an area of large trees that were blown down between Blaine, Ham Lake, Columbus, and Lino Lakes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon of Tuesday, May 29th, thunderstorms began to develop across southwest Minnesota, and move northward. Most of the storms were sub-severe with occasional gusts of 40 to 50 mph noted on airport ASOS/AWOS sites. Other thunderstorms began to develop across south central Minnesota and moved northward toward the Twin Cities area. ||By the mid to late afternoon, temperatures rose into the 90s with CAPE values of 1500-2000 J/Kg. Due to weak wind shear, most of the storms were outflow dominant, but occasional severe wind gusts occurred with the stronger cores. Two storms produced significant wind damage near Geneva, north of Albert Lea, Minnesota, and Hutchinson, Minnesota. A turkey barn was destroyed near Geneva, and three people were injured but recovered from their injuries. Around Hutchinson, there were numerous reports of downed trees and power lines. Some of the trees caused damage to homes.  The local emergency manager in McLeod County determined that there were about 5-6 houses in Hutchinson that received structural damage from trees.||In addition, McLeod County declared a state of emergency on May 30, 2018 as a result of these high winds. An initial damage assessment was conducted and determined that there was about $54,000 in estimated public assistance damages.||Other storms produced an occasional hail stone up to quarter size. However, most of the storms were only producing dime to nickel size hail with wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A violent outbreak of significant straight line winds, downburst winds, and multiple tornadoes occurred during the afternoon and early evening across southern Minnesota. ||During the afternoon of Thursday, September 20th, a warm front lifted northward across far southern Minnesota. By the late afternoon, the warm front lied from just south of the Minnesota River, northeast to around the city of Wabasha. ||Several thunderstorms developed in southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. These storms moved into far southern Minnesota and quickly intensified and raced off to the east-northeast along, and north of this warm front. ||Damage reports started to come into the office around 4 PM CST in Martin County, with multiple reports of significant damage from south of Mankato, to Waseca, then northeast through Waterville, Morristown, Owatonna, Faribault, Northfield, Cannon Falls, and Red Wing before moving into west central Wisconsin. ||A long, and several day storm survey indicated at least XX confirmed tornadoes occurred in a widespread area of damaging winds from Martin County, northeast into west central Wisconsin. ||The most destruction part of the storm occurred around Waterville, Morristown, Owatonna, Faribault and Cannon Falls. These cities all reported homes, buildings and warehouses either damaged or destroyed. ||Hundreds of large, mature trees were uprooted or snapped along this path of destruction, including numerous trees up to 4 feet in diameter. In addition to the trees, structural damage was widespread including the Faribault airport where 80 planes were either damaged or destroyed as hangers were blown off their foundation. ||The towns of Waterville and Faribault had the most homes damaged which was associated with EF2 tornado damage.  ||Two miles west of the Faribault airport, a resort area on Roberds Lake had upwords of 75 percent of the trees uprooted or blown down on the east side of the lake. Some of the trees landed on homes and split them in half.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey, plus drone footage, concluded that an EF1 tornado tracked 1.9 miles which started 3 miles south of Cannon Falls, crossed over Highway 52, and lifted just south of downtown Cannon Falls. This tornado damaged a warehouse building, numerous large trees, homes, and a cemetery. The damage was consistent with EF1 damage with maximum wind speeds of 95 mph and a width of 440 yards. There was other damage to the north of the tornado track which was consistent with downburst winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey, plus areal footage of drone and satellite data, concluded that an EF1 tornado started west of Owatonna, and tracked 6.5 miles across the southern and eastern portions of the town, before lifting east of the Brooktree Golf Course. The damage was consistent with EF1 damage with maximum wind speeds of 90 mph and a width of 300 yards. An outbuilding and silo were damaged near the start of the tornado track, with numerous large trees uprooted or blown down in the city of Owatonna. In addition to the tornado damage, severe straight-line winds from inflow of the tornado caused numerous large blown down trees and power poles on the south side of the track.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the area. Rich, low-level moisture was in place ahead of the front and numerous strong to severe storms developed. Storm motion was west to east parallel to the front and resulted in heavy rain. Storms earlier in the day saturated the soil and contributed to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy runoff led to the collapse of a small culvert near the intersection of Scenic Highway 61 and Alseth Road next to the North Shore Scenic Railroad tracks. This led to a small washout on the railroad.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of severe thunderstorms that developed early Tuesday morning, July 3rd, around Redwood Falls, slowly diminished in intensity, but outflow from these storms, produced severe wind gusts in portions of central Minnesota during the morning. There were no reports of damage, but winds were gusting as high as 70 mph. Southern Benton, southern Mille Lacs, southern Isanti, and northern Sherburne counties all had measured wind gusts of between 45 to 55 mph for an hour or two, with the St. Cloud airport recording a wind gust of 70 mph at 8:44 CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved southeast across southern Minnesota during the afternoon of Thursday, July 12th. Along this front, a line of thunderstorms developed, and moved eastward. Several reports of trees, branches, and power lines were blown down in east central, and south central Minnesota. The worst area was around Red Wing where numerous larger trees were blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down in Excelsior.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were knocked down onto power lines in Knife River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved southeast across southern Minnesota during the afternoon of Thursday, July 12th. Along this front, a line of thunderstorms developed, and moved eastward. Several reports of trees, branches, and power lines were blown down in east central, and south central Minnesota. The worst area was around Red Wing where numerous larger trees were blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down near Flying Cloud airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the evening of September 20th. These storms produced a tornado that briefly moved across the far northwest corner of Wabasha County north of Mazeppa. The storms also occasionally produced some damaging winds with trees blown down west of West Concord (Dodge County) and in Lake City (Wabasha County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This the continuation of a tornado that began in Goodhue County. A NWS Storm Survey team found that the tornado moved briefly across the far northwest corner of Wabasha County north of Mazeppa before moving back into Goodhue County. Damage was limited to crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed late Thursday morning across south central Minnesota. These storms developed along a very apparent west to east wind shift line. Several funnel clouds were reported along this line. One of the stronger storms had enough strength to develop a weak tornado west of Ceylon, Minnesota. The tornado was recorded on video from a local sheriff as it slowly moved across an open farm field. Most of the damage was associated with a row of trees before it dissipated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud picture was sent to the NWS through Twitter. The funnel cloud was located between Sanborn and Springfield on Highway 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying line of thunderstorms that developed across North Dakota during the morning of July 4, 2018, produced an outflow boundary that moved across central and southern Minnesota during the morning. Most of the wind speeds were below severe limits, and some airport wind sensors measured wind gusts of 45 to 50 mph, including Alexandria, Canby, St. Cloud, Crystal and South St. Paul.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 90 foot long portion of a tin, and wood roof from a gas station, was blown off in Morris. There were also power lines blown down around the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the overnight hours of Tuesday, July 3rd, a persistent line of storms, with rainfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour, moved very little from Marshall to Redwood Falls, Olivia, then southward to Tracy, Lamberton, and Springfield, Minnesota. The heaviest rainfall held over the Redwood, and Cottonwood river basin for several hours. Several of the flash flooding parameters, including flash flood guidance, and return period for a 6 hour rainfall event, were extreme. The suggested return period for such an event in six hours was well over 200 years. ||Over 50 township and country roads were affected by the flood waters. Due to the numerous county roads and bridges that were impassable, the national guard was called in to help those who were isolated due to flood waters. ||By the time the rainfall tapered off Tuesday morning, rainfall amounts ranged from a measured high of 10.50 inches near Wabasso, to several measured amounts of 5 to 9 inches from Belview and Delhi, south and southwest to Redwood Falls, Lucan, to around Walnut Grove, and Lamberton. ||In addition to the flash flooding that inundated the area,  the heavy rains that fell on July 3 caused major flooding on rivers in southwest Minnesota. The Cottonwood River at New Ulm had its second highest crest of record, with 19.64 feet on July 6th. The record crest there is 20.86 feet set on April 8, 1965. The Redwood River at Marshall set a record crest of 17.41 feet on July 3, breaking the old record of 17.09 feet from September 25, 2010. There was extensive flooding in Redwood county with basements flooding and water closing roads. Tracy, Lamberton and Springfield were some of the hardest hit towns due to road closures and detours from flooded farmland. ||Another unfortunate cause from the flooding was an apparent drowning. An individual's car was found completely underwater in a ditch in Hector Township. The vehicle was unoccupied when it was pulled from the water. The body from the vehicle was found two days later in a ditch upstream from the location of the car. ||Governor Dayton declared an emergency for several counties hit by this storm, and storms from the previous month including; Blue Earth, Brown, Faribault, Freeborn, Martin, Mille Lacs, Nicollet, Redwood, Renville, Sibley, Stearns, Stevens, Waseca and Watonwan counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of flash flooding occurred across Redwood County from Belview in the far north, to Walnut Grove and Lamberton in the far south. The town of Wabasso had so much water that baseball fields were covered in a few inches of water. Over 50 township and county roads were impassable during the height of the flood waters. Once the flash flooding part of the storm abated, river flooding took over with river cresting to near or record levels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted through Wisconsin the night of the 26th and into the morning of the 27th, leaving a moist and unstable airmass across the region. A cold front in eastern Minnesota pushing east during the afternoon hours provided the lift to initiate storms. The storms caused street flooding and wind damage in the Sandstone area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported covering a few roads in the Sandstone area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm advection ahead of a cold front moving from the Red River Valley into central Minnesota through the day lead to the development of storms by the late afternoon.  Large hail and strong winds were seen with these storms as they moved through the region. The winds knocked down a tree onto a trailer home, seriously injuring a young girl inside. Additional storms formed later in the evening as the cold front moved in.

A violent outbreak of significant straight line winds, downburst winds, and multiple tornadoes occurred during the afternoon and early evening across southern Minnesota. ||During the afternoon of Thursday, September 20th, a warm front lifted northward across far southern Minnesota. By the late afternoon, the warm front lied from just south of the Minnesota River, northeast to around the city of Wabasha. ||Several thunderstorms developed in southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. These storms moved into far southern Minnesota and quickly intensified and raced off to the east-northeast along, and north of this warm front. ||Damage reports started to come into the office around 4 PM CST in Martin County, with multiple reports of significant damage from south of Mankato, to Waseca, then northeast through Waterville, Morristown, Owatonna, Faribault, Northfield, Cannon Falls, and Red Wing before moving into west central Wisconsin. ||A long, and several day storm survey indicated at least XX confirmed tornadoes occurred in a widespread area of damaging winds from Martin County, northeast into west central Wisconsin. ||The most destruction part of the storm occurred around Waterville, Morristown, Owatonna, Faribault and Cannon Falls. These cities all reported homes, buildings and warehouses either damaged or destroyed. ||Hundreds of large, mature trees were uprooted or snapped along this path of destruction, including numerous trees up to 4 feet in diameter. In addition to the trees, structural damage was widespread including the Faribault airport where 80 planes were either damaged or destroyed as hangers were blown off their foundation. ||The towns of Waterville and Faribault had the most homes damaged which was associated with EF2 tornado damage.  ||Two miles west of the Faribault airport, a resort area on Roberds Lake had upwords of 75 percent of the trees uprooted or blown down on the east side of the lake. Some of the trees landed on homes and split them in half.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey, plus aerial pictures and satellite imagery, concluded that an EF2 tornado started southwest of the Faribault airport, near the intersection of 195th Street West and Cedar Lake Blvd, and tracked 3.0 miles northeast. It destroyed parts of the Faribault airport before lifting just north of the airport grounds. Numerous hangers were either damaged or destroyed. ||The 125 mph winds were estimated based on a well built hanger destroyed and blown off its foundation. Footings were also ripped from its frame. Local officials estimated that 80 airplanes were damaged or destroyed at the airport. Some of the debris was blown across the highway on the north side of the airport.||There was also damage on the southeast side of the tornado track which was consistent with downburst and inflow winds. These winds were estimated up to 85 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm advection ahead of a cold front moving from the Red River Valley into central Minnesota through the day lead to the development of storms by the late afternoon.  Large hail and strong winds were seen with these storms as they moved through the region. The winds knocked down a tree onto a trailer home, seriously injuring a young girl inside. Additional storms formed later in the evening as the cold front moved in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail shattered the back window of a vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted across northern Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening hours. A low-level jet and associated warm air advection led to the development of isolated storms that contained severe hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail covered the ground.

During the overnight hours of Tuesday, July 3rd, a persistent line of storms, with rainfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour, moved very little from Marshall to Redwood Falls, Olivia, then southward to Tracy, Lamberton, and Springfield, Minnesota. The heaviest rainfall held over the Redwood, and Cottonwood river basin for several hours. Several of the flash flooding parameters, including flash flood guidance, and return period for a 6 hour rainfall event, were extreme. The suggested return period for such an event in six hours was well over 200 years. ||Over 50 township and country roads were affected by the flood waters. Due to the numerous county roads and bridges that were impassable, the national guard was called in to help those who were isolated due to flood waters. ||By the time the rainfall tapered off Tuesday morning, rainfall amounts ranged from a measured high of 10.50 inches near Wabasso, to several measured amounts of 5 to 9 inches from Belview and Delhi, south and southwest to Redwood Falls, Lucan, to around Walnut Grove, and Lamberton. ||In addition to the flash flooding that inundated the area,  the heavy rains that fell on July 3 caused major flooding on rivers in southwest Minnesota. The Cottonwood River at New Ulm had its second highest crest of record, with 19.64 feet on July 6th. The record crest there is 20.86 feet set on April 8, 1965. The Redwood River at Marshall set a record crest of 17.41 feet on July 3, breaking the old record of 17.09 feet from September 25, 2010. There was extensive flooding in Redwood county with basements flooding and water closing roads. Tracy, Lamberton and Springfield were some of the hardest hit towns due to road closures and detours from flooded farmland. ||Another unfortunate cause from the flooding was an apparent drowning. An individual's car was found completely underwater in a ditch in Hector Township. The vehicle was unoccupied when it was pulled from the water. The body from the vehicle was found two days later in a ditch upstream from the location of the car. ||Governor Dayton declared an emergency for several counties hit by this storm, and storms from the previous month including; Blue Earth, Brown, Faribault, Freeborn, Martin, Mille Lacs, Nicollet, Redwood, Renville, Sibley, Stearns, Stevens, Waseca and Watonwan counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured rainfall report of 4.55 inches fell west of Lucan in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary west to east frontal boundary was located across Nebraska and Iowa for several days. With an extremely moist atmosphere featuring near record precipitable water in place, several weak disturbances resulted in prolific rainfall from 3 to as much as 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop and pasture land was inundated, as well as portions of the Flandreau Golf Course, as levels on Flandreau Creek above Flandreau climbed to 1.1 feet above flood stage (gage site FCRS2) on September 21. The river returned quickly below flood stage on September 22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm advection ahead of a cold front moving from the Red River Valley into central Minnesota through the day lead to the development of storms by the late afternoon.  Large hail and strong winds were seen with these storms as they moved through the region. The winds knocked down a tree onto a trailer home, seriously injuring a young girl inside. Additional storms formed later in the evening as the cold front moved in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and power lines were downed by the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary west to east frontal boundary was located across Nebraska and Iowa for several days. With an extremely moist atmosphere featuring near record precipitable water in place, several weak disturbances resulted in prolific rainfall from 3 to as much as 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall on September 18-20 brought a widespread 3 to 5 inches of rainfall with 4.66 inches at Pipestone. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon of September 17th. These storms mainly produced wind gusts in the 40 to 50 mph range, which was enough to cause some damage. Trees and power lines were blown down in Dodge Center (Dodge County), damaged a building roof in Goodview (Winona County) and blew down numerous trees in the city of Winona (Winona County). The storms also dropped about 2 inches of rain in less than hour in Winona that caused some street flooding to occur.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms that developed in South Dakota Monday morning, September 17th, quickly moved eastward into western and southern Minnesota during the afternoon. Most of the damaging winds were associated with the leading edge of a strong outflow boundary that caused numerous trees and power lines to be blown down from Eagan, Apple Valley, Rosemount, Lakeville and Farmington in Dakota County southward to Northfield, Lonsdale and Morristown in Rice County.||Behind this leading edge of strong winds, thunderstorms developed, but most of these were non-severe and producing occasional gusty winds and heavy rainfall. One storm did produced a 51 knot wind gust at the New Ulm airport from thunderstorms later in the afternoon.

A complex of thunderstorms that developed in South Dakota Monday morning, September 17th, quickly moved eastward into western and southern Minnesota during the afternoon. EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several reports of strong winds that were associated with an outflow boundary from thunderstorms. These winds produced downed power lines and trees from Eagan, Apple Valley, Rosemount, Lakeville to Farmington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary west to east frontal boundary was located across Nebraska and Iowa for several days. With an extremely moist atmosphere featuring near record precipitable water in place, several weak disturbances resulted in prolific rainfall from 3 to as much as 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall on September 18-20 brought a widespread 3 to 5 inches of rainfall with 4.66 inches at Pipestone. Moderate flooding occurred with crop and pasture land inundated, as well as the low water crossing on 250th Street near the South Dakota border, as Split Rock Creek below Jasper (gage site JSPM5) crested 4.6 feet above flood stage at 0200LST on September 21, with a lesser secondary crest at 2200LST on September 21. The river dropped below flood stage on September 26.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused flooding to occur across portions of Wabasha County. Water was over parts of State Highway 60 and some secondary roads had washouts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front pushed across northern Minnesota during the early morning hours of July 2nd. A few thunderstorms developed ahead of the front overnight and a few contained stronger wind gusts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water covered the road at the intersection of US Highway 2 and Minnesota Highway 6 in Deer River.

Tornado damage was consistent with EF1 damage with winds up to 100 mph, and a maximum path width of 300 yards. Damage was confined to trees, but a silo was also damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado moved across rural portions of far northeast Fillmore County into southwest Winona County. The tornado started northwest of Rushford (Fillmore County) and was on the ground for about 5 minutes causing minor damage to a house and trees. Several rounds of thunderstorms moved across the area dumping heavy rain. The heavy caused street flooding to occur in Rochester (Olmsted County) with cars becoming trapped in the flood waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain caused street flooding to occur in portions of Rochester. Cars became stalled in the flood waters on the northwest side of the city. Part of an intersection of two city streets was partially washed out. Flooding also occurred in Byron where roads were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon of September 17th. These storms mainly produced wind gusts in the 40 to 50 mph range, which was enough to cause some damage. Trees and power lines were blown down in Dodge Center (Dodge County), damaged a building roof in Goodview (Winona County) and blew down numerous trees in the city of Winona (Winona County). The storms also dropped about 2 inches of rain in less than hour in Winona that caused some street flooding to occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down and onto power lines in Dodge Center. The automated weather observing equipment at the airport measured a wind gust of 51 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding persisted through the night and early morning hours across much of Southwest Minnesota in Lyon, Murray and northwest portions of Cottonwood county after heavy rainfall from the prior early morning.  Impacts continued to feature homes getting water into the lower levels and flooded roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S Highway 14 remained closed east of Tracy with water ponded over the roadway. Numerous basements in Marshall were flooded and city streets remained impassable due to standing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of July 4th, surface low pressure had set up over north central South Dakota, with an east-to-west warm front extending eastward from the low along the North Dakota/South Dakota border into west central Minnesota. Temperatures across eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota ranged in the 70s with dew points in the upper 60s to low 70s. The mid level winds (500mb) were from the south-southwest. A bowing line of thunderstorms, stretching from south of Rugby to the North Dakota/South Dakota border moved into portions of the southwest Devils Lake region just after 130 am CDT. Following the mid level wind flow, the storms tracked to the east-northeast, reaching the Red River Valley around 4 am CDT. At this point, the bowing line of storms stretched from southern Manitoba down into northeast South Dakota. This bowing line of storms tracked through the northwest quarter of Minnesota in the 4 to 6 am CDT time frame, so these storms were moving quite quickly. Most of the storms had exited the Baudette to Park Rapids corridor by 7 am CDT. These storms produced widespread damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado occurred west of Bemidji State University. A garage was lifted off its foundation, trees were snapped or uprooted, and several roofs were damaged. Projectiles damaged many windows in the area. Flying debris damaged light poles and street signs in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across northern Minnesota. A few storms were strong to severe and featured damaging winds and hail. Saturated grounds from previous rainfall led to some minor flash flooding across the area as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just after midnight, or during the early morning hours of July 8th, a stationary boundary was draped from the northern Red River Valley back to south of Bismarck. South of the boundary, temperatures were still in the lower 80s with dew points in the lower 70s. Just after 3 am CDT, an area of thunderstorms moved into the New Rockford, ND, area, while another thunderstorm formed around Thief River Falls, MN. Both areas of storms moved east-northeast, keeping most of the thunderstorm activity along the U. S. Highway 2 corridor. Other than a few reports of large hail, these storms primarily produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof panels and roofing material was peeled up from a steel clad pole shed and flung over 100 yards to the east-southeast. Nearby farmsteads across northern Huntsville and southern Sullivan townships had large tree branches blown down and had shingles and gutters torn off the houses and sheds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the northwest quarter of Minnesota during the evening of the 11th into the early morning hours of the 12th. Ahead of this cold front, temperatures over the northwest quarter of Minnesota during the early evening of the 11th ranged well into the 80s, with dew points in the middle 70s, so it was very warm and muggy. Thunderstorms broke out along the front, producing funnel clouds, tornadoes, large hail, and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of this tornado, along with video, were posted to social media. The tornado touched down just off the shore, over Lower Red Lake. It moved on shore roughly two miles west of Ponemah and continued to track to the east-northeast, for another mile or so, before dissipating. Large poplar and ash tree limbs were broken down along this path. Peak winds were estimated at 80 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down and tracked along and across the Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA) 25 road for about two miles, to near the south shore of the Lower Red Lake. Trees were torn up quite violently and debris was spread across nearly a mile of BIA25. Peak winds were estimated at 100 mph.

The tornado tracked onshore about four miles north-northwest of Battle River, and continued on an intermittent east-northeast track for nearly five miles, crossing Beltrami County 23 about four miles north of Saum. Large poplar and pine trees were snapped or uprooted along the damage path. Peak winds were estimated at 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms flared up across southern Cass County (North Dakota) on the afternoon of August 26th, dropping some large hail. After a several hour break, another storm intensified and dropped large hail over north central Becker and southern Mahnomen counties in Minnesota. Then after 8 pm CDT, a line of storms from north central North Dakota spread into the Devils Lake region. These storms produced damaging wind gusts. After 9 pm CDT, another line of storms formed ahead of the Devils Lake line, and these also tracked to the east. This line brought hail and damaging wind gusts to the Valley City and Grand Forks areas. Eventually, both lines of storms moved into the central and northern Red River Valley, then progressed into northwest Minnesota. As they did, they continued to produce damaging winds. Within the lines, several long track tornadoes also formed. Both of these occurred from the northeast corner of Norman County up toward the Lower Red Lake in Minnesota. Quite a bit of tree damage resulted along this track.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust was measured by Minnesota DOT RWIS sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tree fire was started in Winona (Winona County) from a lightning strike. As thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the early afternoon of August 28th, lightning struck a tree, setting it on fire and and also ruptured a gas line. The fire was not extinguished until the utility company shut the gas off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike started a tree fire and ruptured a gas line in Winona. The fire burned until the utility company shut the gas off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed ahead of a northward moving warm front during the afternoon and were being fed from the south by unseasonably moist air. Most of the storms remained sub-severe, but one storm evolved into a supercell with large hail. These storms then moved into Wisconsin and were training along the warm front, leading to a flash flood threat. The cold front then approached the region overnight and led to a line of storms that ripped up numerous trees across northern Minnesota.

A strong upper-level wave and jet entrance region pushed into the Northern Plains, with a tight mid-level frontal boundary stalling across the area for several hours within confluent southwest flow aloft. Unseasonably cold air rushed into the region behind a southward surging low-level frontal boundary, with developing precipitation making a very rapid transition from rain to snow. Snow accumulated quickly in the overnight and early morning hours due to proximity of slightly unstable air aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to occasionally moderate snow accumulated to 2 to 6 inches across much of  southwest Minnesota. Snowfall of 3.0 inches was measured at Pipestone and near Edgerton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon of Monday, August, 27th, several complexes of storms moved across South Dakota, Nebraska, northwest Iowa, and into western and southern Minnesota. The complex of storms in South Dakota produced large hail in west central Minnesota, or mainly around the communities of Glenwood, Alexandria, and Sauk Centre. A more pronounced area of storms that developed in northeast Nebraska, and into northwest Iowa, moved east-northeast into south central Minnesota. Several reports of wind damage occurred around Welcome, Truman and Northrop.  These storms became more organized as they moved just south of the Twin Cities Metro area where two tornadoes  formed between Faribault and Red Wing. These clusters of storms developed into a bow echo as it raced northeast into west central Wisconsin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power lines were blown down in Jordan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms flared up across southern Cass County (North Dakota) on the afternoon of August 26th, dropping some large hail. After a several hour break, another storm intensified and dropped large hail over north central Becker and southern Mahnomen counties in Minnesota. Then after 8 pm CDT, a line of storms from north central North Dakota spread into the Devils Lake region. These storms produced damaging wind gusts. After 9 pm CDT, another line of storms formed ahead of the Devils Lake line, and these also tracked to the east. This line brought hail and damaging wind gusts to the Valley City and Grand Forks areas. Eventually, both lines of storms moved into the central and northern Red River Valley, then progressed into northwest Minnesota. As they did, they continued to produce damaging winds. Within the lines, several long track tornadoes also formed. Both of these occurred from the northeast corner of Norman County up toward the Lower Red Lake in Minnesota. Quite a bit of tree damage resulted along this track.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter sized hail fell with very heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon north of a warm front across far southern South Dakota as a weak disturbances moved eastward across the state. As storms shifted into southwest Minnesota during the evening, the slow-moving and training storms produced spotty heavy rainfall and localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several vehicles were stalled out within the Jasper city limits due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening of August 27th. Winds from these storms damaged trees in Kasson (Dodge County). Storms also reformed over the same area which led to extensive flash flooding, especially closer to the Wisconsin border.  In Hokah (Houston County), flooding from the Root River went over State Highway 16 and Como Falls was destroyed from flooding along Thompson Creek. There were numerous mudslides and secondary roads damaged, along with culverts and other public infrastructure.  A state of emergency was declared in Houston County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were damaged in Kasson.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey determined that a tornado hit a farm east of Nerstrand. Several trees were downed, and a shed was destroyed. The wind also blew down a power pole, landing on a barn which then caught on fire.  The tornado may have started southwest of Nerstrand, but it was impossible to determine from the damage pattern. Maximum winds were estimated at around 70 mph.

Two windows were blown in at a nearby school, which also had a small portion of its roof torn back. Numerous trees were broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms between Bismarck and Jamestown (North Dakota) during the mid evening of August 25th tracked eastward along the Interstate 94 corridor during the late evening and into the early morning hours of August 26th. The storms dropped large hail over portions of Clay County (Minnesota) before weakening again.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail and very heavy rain fell across eastern Alliance and western Barnesville townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite the fact that temperatures over southeast North Dakota at 7 pm CDT on August 27th were only around 60 degrees, with dew points in the lower 50s, a few severe thunderstorms drifted across portions of southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota. These storms produced large hail and brief heavy rain.

Despite the fact that temperatures over southeast North Dakota at 7 pm CDT on August 27th were only around 60 degrees, with dew points in the lower 50s, a few severe thunderstorms drifted across portions of southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota. These storms produced large hail and brief heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters covered the ground, filled ditches, and damaged crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around 1 pm CDT on August 31st, a line of thunderstorms took shape between Roseau and Grand Forks. As these storms moved east of the Thief River Falls area, they increased in intensity and dropped large hail. As the line progressed through eastern Lake of the Woods County, another segment of the line strengthened and dropped large hail. A little over an hour later, just after 4 pm CDT, another cluster of storms formed over extreme southeast North Dakota. These storms tracked east-northeast, through the Fairmount (North Dakota) area, and the Fergus Falls, Battle Lake, and Wadena (Minnesota) areas. These storms also produced large hail. A lone thunderstorm cell formed over south central Grand Forks County (North Dakota) around 830 pm CDT. This storm expanded into a cluster of storms as it progressed eastward into western Polk County (Minnesota). Finally, just before midnight CDT, another lone thunderstorm just southeast of Roseau also dropped large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter sized hail fell.

In Hokah (Houston County), flooding from the Root River went over State Highway 16 and Como Falls was destroyed from flooding along Thompson Creek. There were numerous mudslides and secondary roads damaged, along with culverts and other public infrastructure.  A state of emergency was declared in Houston County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall totaled 6.50 inches during this time frame.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of storms that developed across northeast South Dakota Monday afternoon, August 6th, moved east-southeast across western Minnesota. Most of the storms were not severe, but one storm that moved near Paynesville, Minnesota produced wind damage. The main damage was associated with large trees uprooted on the south side of Paynesville, with some of the debris thrown out into a school parking lot.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small area of intense downburst winds occurred on the south side of Paynesville. The local airport (KPEX), recorded a measured wind gust of 56 mph at 5:35 PM CST. Several trees were uprooted, and the debris was spread across a school parking lot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms that developed across South Dakota, moved eastward into west central, central and east central Minnesota during the late afternoon, and evening hours. Several bow echo segments were indicated on radar, with one producing a tornado between Atwater and Grove City. A few trees and power lines were blown down as this line of storms continued to move eastward across Minnesota. ||There were numerous measured wind gusts in the range of 45 to 55 mph as these storms moved across the state, including 55 mph at the St. Paul airport, 53 mph near Starbuck, and Granite Falls, 51 mph near Clearwater, 49 mph near Clara City, 48 mph at the Minneapolis, St. Paul International Airport, and 47 mph at the Madison airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is the continuation of the same EF0 tornado that tracked across Kandiyohi county, and entered Meeker county. This tornado tracked an additional 9.5 miles east along Highway 12, through Grove City, to just northwest of Litchfield.  Pockets of tree damage, an overturned trailer, and some other minor structural damage to sheds and pole barns resulted. Straight line wind damage, mostly to trees, was found south of the tornado |track and Highway 12. Maximum winds were estimated at 80 mph, with a width of 300 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system that was rotating across the central part of the United States, developed small clusters of storms on the northwest side of the circulation. One of these storms developed in southern Steele county and produced wind damage near Ellendale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were blown down in the town of Ellendale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening of August 1st. These storms dropped penny sized hail in Spring Grove (Houston County).

Around 1 pm CDT on August 31st, a line of thunderstorms took shape between Roseau and Grand Forks. As these storms moved east of the Thief River Falls area, they increased in intensity and dropped large hail. As the line progressed through eastern Lake of the Woods County, another segment of the line strengthened and dropped large hail. A little over an hour later, just after 4 pm CDT, another cluster of storms formed over extreme southeast North Dakota. These storms tracked east-northeast, through the Fairmount (North Dakota) area, and the Fergus Falls, Battle Lake, and Wadena (Minnesota) areas. These storms also produced large hail. A lone thunderstorm cell formed over south central Grand Forks County (North Dakota) around 830 pm CDT. This storm expanded into a cluster of storms as it progressed eastward into western Polk County (Minnesota). Finally, just before midnight CDT, another lone thunderstorm just southeast of Roseau also dropped large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail fell at the Fiske Lake public access.

Thunderstorms flared up across southern Cass County (North Dakota) on the afternoon of August 26th, dropping some large hail. After a several hour break, another storm intensified and dropped large hail over north central Becker and southern Mahnomen counties in Minnesota. Then after 8 pm CDT, a line of storms from north central North Dakota spread into the Devils Lake region. These storms produced damaging wind gusts. After 9 pm CDT, another line of storms formed ahead of the Devils Lake line, and these also tracked to the east. This line brought hail and damaging wind gusts to the Valley City and Grand Forks areas. Eventually, both lines of storms moved into the central and northern Red River Valley, then progressed into northwest Minnesota. As they did, they continued to produce damaging winds. Within the lines, several long track tornadoes also formed. Both of these occurred from the northeast corner of Norman County up toward the Lower Red Lake in Minnesota. Quite a bit of tree damage resulted along this track.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and branches were blown down near the Northwood Church.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper-level jet streak moved into southeast South Dakota during the late evening of August 4, a cluster of storms developed in south central South Dakota along and in advance of a surface trough axis where elevated instability was over 2000 J/kg. These storms moved in a general easterly direction, but weakened while progressing into southwest Minnesota. One storm spiked in intensity near Slayton and produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak gust was measured at the Slayton Municipal Airport. Time estimated from radar, as occurred during the 10 minutes prior to 0159LST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado tracked intermittently from 3 miles north-northeast of Fosston to 9 miles north-northeast of Leonard (in Clearwater County), a total of about 26 miles. Hundreds of trees were damaged, uprooted, or snapped along the track. Numerous cabins, farm outbuildings, houses, campers, boats, and crops were also damaged. The tornado was likely wrapped in downburst winds and rain at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon north of a warm front across far southern South Dakota as a weak disturbances moved eastward across the state. As storms shifted into southwest Minnesota during the evening, the slow-moving and training storms produced spotty heavy rainfall and localized flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two disturbances sliding across the Northern Plains resulted in fairly widespread moderate rainfall from October 9-11. Another episode of minor to moderate flooding ensued with rain totaling from 1.50 to 2.00 inches throughout the basin on saturated soils.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms between Bismarck and Jamestown (North Dakota) during the mid evening of August 25th tracked eastward along the Interstate 94 corridor during the late evening and into the early morning hours of August 26th. The storms dropped large hail over portions of Clay County (Minnesota) before weakening again.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed ahead of a northward moving warm front during the afternoon and were being fed from the south by unseasonably moist air. Most of the storms remained sub-severe, but one storm evolved into a supercell with large hail. These storms then moved into Wisconsin and were training along the warm front, leading to a flash flood threat. The cold front then approached the region overnight and led to a line of storms that ripped up numerous trees across northern Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large trees were uprooted and snapped in International Falls leading to multiple power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon of Monday, August, 27th, several complexes of storms moved across South Dakota, Nebraska, northwest Iowa, and into western and southern Minnesota. The complex of storms in South Dakota produced large hail in west central Minnesota, or mainly around the communities of Glenwood, Alexandria, and Sauk Centre. A more pronounced area of storms that developed in northeast Nebraska, and into northwest Iowa, moved east-northeast into south central Minnesota. Several reports of wind damage occurred around Welcome, Truman and Northrop.  These storms became more organized as they moved just south of the Twin Cities Metro area where two tornadoes  formed between Faribault and Red Wing. These clusters of storms developed into a bow echo as it raced northeast into west central Wisconsin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The early morning hours of Friday, June 29th, remained warm and muggy. Just after midnight, temperatures were still in the 70s with dew points around 70. A line of thunderstorms moved into the Devils Lake area around 2 am CDT. This line of storms had several bowing segments, which are indicative of strong winds. The most obvious bowing segment between highway 2 and Interstate 94, where the most amount of damage occurred. The main bowing segment exited into north central Minnesota around 6 am CDT. Well behind the bowing thunderstorm complex, a mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) formed, and produced another round of widespread damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down, some falling on a house and a driveway. The power was also knocked out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms began in western North Dakota early in the evening of June 28th and then swept into northern Minnesota during the early morning hours of the 29th. While there were a few hail reports, this line was primarily a damaging wind producer and featured a measured gust to 80 mph. Numerous trees were damaged by the storms and fell onto homes and power lines.The storms continued to produce strong winds over the open waters of Lake Superior, but weakened to the south as they moved into northwestern Wisconsin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was draped across the southern and southwestern reaches of the CWA during the day. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel-sized hail fell on the far south side of Grand Rapids.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was located across central Minnesota into southwest Wisconsin. Low level flow maintained the influx of moisture and warm air as a shortwave topped a ridge in place to the west. This led to storms throughout much of the day, a few of which were severe with damaging winds and hail. The storms also contained heavy rainfall which primed parts of the area for major flash flooding the following night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The tail end of an overnight MCS brought scattered thunderstorms to southwest Minnesota during the very early morning hours of June 6.  An environment of moderate elevated instability resulted in the strongest storm producing marginally large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was accompanied by 50 mph winds which devastated a small area of crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving into the Red River Valley along with attendant warm and cold fronts, led to the development of storms in eastern North Dakota and western Minnesota the evening of the 29th. These storms then pushed east south of the warm front across northern Minnesota during the early morning hours of the 30th. The line of storms featured a bow echo leading to numerous wind damage reports. Lightning also struck a cabin injuring a person sleeping inside the cabin.

An increasing low-level jet of 40 knots with terminus across northwest Iowa spurred development of thunderstorms during the early morning hours. Mid-level lapse rates were at best weakly unstable from 7-7.5 C/km resulting in modest elevated instability of 1000-1500 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intermittent hail resulted in dime to half dollar size hail nearly covering the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed north of a surface warm front in northeast Nebraska and west central Iowa during the early evening. With 2500 J/kg elevated CAPE, updrafts were strong enough to produce spotty large hail.

Thunderstorms formed over eastern North Dakota during the mid afternoon of June 8th and spread eastward into the Red River Valley by the mid to late afternoon and early evening. These storms produced a few weak tornadoes and strong wind gusts. Just north of Reynolds (ND), two semi trucks were blown over, which garnered a lot of media interest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few 4 to 6 inch diameter tree branches were blown down in a farm yard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After the passage of a large bowing thunderstorm complex, strong gradient winds developed in the wake. These strong winds were associated with a mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) that formed in the stratiform rain area behind the squall line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were blown down in a farm shelterbelt six miles west of Stephen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms intensified over portions of Hubbard County during the early morning hours of June 30th.

Numerous outflow boundaries from overnight convection re-fired across southwest Minnesota around mid afternoon as diurnal heating weakened the cap and increased MLCAPE to around 2000 J/kg. The leading edge of larger-scale lift began to move in late afternoon with jet streak punching toward central Nebraska ahead of a deep western U.S. trough, but overall deep-layer shear remained quite weak with right-moving and mean storm motion vector magnitudes at 10 knots or less northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms dumped an estimated 2 to 4 inches rainfall in a couple of hours.  Roads near the intersection of 141st St. and 170th Ave. were inundated by a couple feet of flowing water from a nearby creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous outflow boundaries from overnight convection re-fired across southwest Minnesota around mid afternoon as diurnal heating weakened the cap and increased MLCAPE to around 2000 J/kg. The leading edge of larger-scale lift began to move in late afternoon with jet streak punching toward central Nebraska ahead of a deep western U.S. trough, but overall deep-layer shear remained quite weak with right-moving and mean storm motion vector magnitudes at 10 knots or less northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 2 to 4 inches of rainfall over two hours resulted in water over Highway 59 north of Slayton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third and final day of a three day severe weather event across the Northland. A stalled out frontal system finally made a push across the area leading to another round of severe storms and heavy rainfall over areas that had seen dramatic flash flooding the previous night. Widespread rainfall amounts of 1-2 inches plus over northeastern Minnesota and northwestern Wisconsin sent already swollen creeks and rivers out of their banks and over roadways.

Thunderstorms formed over eastern North Dakota during the mid afternoon of June 8th and spread eastward into the Red River Valley by the mid to late afternoon and early evening. These storms produced a few weak tornadoes and strong wind gusts. Just north of Reynolds (ND), two semi trucks were blown over, which garnered a lot of media interest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter sized hail fell with heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A law enforcement officer out on patrol noticed a funnel cloud near Carp.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An officer reported that a funnel came about halfway down before it dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bow shaped complex of thunderstorms lifted north-northeast out of northeast South Dakota and produced several reports of strong winds over southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind speeds were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of June 16th, a weak frontal boundary stretched north-northeast from Watertown in South Dakota, up through Wadena and International Falls in Minnesota. Along the boundary in west central Minnesota, surface dew points held in the lower 70s with precipitable water values around 1.50 inches. Thunderstorms flared up during the evening and produced a few damaging wind gusts in addition to a band of four to seven inches of rain. This heavy rain band generally ran from north of Breckenridge to between Park Rapids and Wadena in Minnesota. This heavy rain resulted in quite a few reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple 2 to 3 inch diameter tree branches were blown down. The power was also out for about three hours after the storm passed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of June 9th. These storms produced locally heavy rains with the runoff resulting in flash flooding across Fillmore County. Several rural roads were washed out or underwater near Harmony across the western and southern sections of the county including State Highway 16 east of Spring Valley. A deputy sheriff was stranded by flood waters near Granger before moving to higher ground and finding an exit. The highest reported rainfall total was 6 inches in Spring Valley. The Fillmore County Board of Supervisors declared a state of emergency with estimated damages of over $200,000 in Bristol and Forestville Townships.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported northwest of Eitzen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed over southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon of June 15th. These storms dropped some hail before moving into Wisconsin. Dime sized hail fell on the northwest side of Rochester and nickel sized hail was reported on the north side of Rochester (Olmsted County).

Primed by 1 to 3 inches of rainfall on June 17-18, starting June 20, the area was impacted adversely by several very slow moving mid-level waves which drifted across the northern and central Plains during the following week. High precipitable water and persistent lift forcing resulted in a widespread 4 to 8 inches of rainfall, inundating crop and pasture land as well as several roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A week of periodic heavy rainfall ending June 26 resulted in a widespread 4 to 8 inches of rainfall, much of which occurred on June 20 and 24. Weekly rainfall totals included 7.30 inches at Lake Wilson and 5.60 inches at Windom. Moderate flooding occurred with significant amounts of crop and pasture land inundated, as well as numerous township and county roads and Island Park in Windom, as the Des Moines River at Windom (gage site WDOM5) crested 2.9 feet above flood stage on June 29. The river remained in moderate flood stage at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The early morning hours of Friday, June 29th, remained warm and muggy. Just after midnight, temperatures were still in the 70s with dew points around 70. A line of thunderstorms moved into the Devils Lake area around 2 am CDT. This line of storms had several bowing segments, which are indicative of strong winds. The most obvious bowing segment between highway 2 and Interstate 94, where the most amount of damage occurred. The main bowing segment exited into north central Minnesota around 6 am CDT. Well behind the bowing thunderstorm complex, a mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) formed, and produced another round of widespread damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree, knocking down several branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Somewhat unexpectedly, one very strong thunderstorm tracked across north central North Dakota into the northern Devils Lake region during the late afternoon of June 14th, dropping large hail and heavy rain. Several hours later, more thunderstorms across southern Manitoba moved back into the same area north of Devils Lake. These storms produced several tornadoes, strong winds, and large hail. Strong storms also fired up across southeast North Dakota around midnight, which tracked toward the Fargo-Moorhead area in the early morning hours of the June 15th.

The early morning hours of Friday, June 29th, remained warm and muggy. Just after midnight, temperatures were still in the 70s with dew points around 70. A line of thunderstorms moved into the Devils Lake area around 2 am CDT. This line of storms had several bowing segments, which are indicative of strong winds. The most obvious bowing segment between highway 2 and Interstate 94, where the most amount of damage occurred. The main bowing segment exited into north central Minnesota around 6 am CDT. Well behind the bowing thunderstorm complex, a mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) formed, and produced another round of widespread damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches were blown down in most farmsteads and shelterbelts across eastern Fisher and western Lowell townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two lines of severe thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of June 30th. These storms dropped some hail and also produced damaging winds. Quarter sized hail fell near Preston (Fillmore County) and Winona (Winona County). A 69 mph wind gust occurred at the Rochester airport (Olmsted County) and trees and power lines were blown down in Austin (Mower County) and Lanesboro (Fillmore County). A lightning strike started a house fire in Goodview (Winona County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell south of Winona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rock slide occurred just south of Winona (Winona County) after thunderstorms with heavy rain moved across the area during the early morning hours of June 18th. A car was damaged by the rock slide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car was damaged by a rock slide along U.S. Highway 61 near Winona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around the noon hour of Thursday, June 28th, an east to west stationary boundary was located across southern North Dakota into central Minnesota. It was warm and muggy, with temperatures in the 80s and dew points in the low 70s along the boundary. Thunderstorms tracking from west to east along highway 2 became severe over the Grand Forks-East Grand Forks area just after the noon hour CDT, dropping heavy rain and large hail. During the afternoon, other storms flared up, mainly between highways 2 and 200.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter sized hail fell.

Thunderstorms tracking from west to east along highway 2 became severe over the Grand Forks-East Grand Forks area just after the noon hour CDT, dropping heavy rain and large hail. During the afternoon, other storms flared up, mainly between highways 2 and 200.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage was reported in the town of Climax. A few minutes of pea to penny sized hail also fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around the noon hour of Thursday, June 28th, an east to west stationary boundary was located across southern North Dakota into central Minnesota. It was warm and muggy, with temperatures in the 80s and dew points in the low 70s along the boundary. Thunderstorms tracking from west to east along highway 2 became severe over the Grand Forks-East Grand Forks area just after the noon hour CDT, dropping heavy rain and large hail. During the afternoon, other storms flared up, mainly between highways 2 and 200.

The early morning hours of Friday, June 29th, remained warm and muggy. Just after midnight, temperatures were still in the 70s with dew points around 70. A line of thunderstorms moved into the Devils Lake area around 2 am CDT. This line of storms had several bowing segments, which are indicative of strong winds. The most obvious bowing segment between highway 2 and Interstate 94, where the most amount of damage occurred. The main bowing segment exited into north central Minnesota around 6 am CDT. Well behind the bowing thunderstorm complex, a mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) formed, and produced another round of widespread damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees and a fence were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rock slide occurred just south of Winona (Winona County) after thunderstorms with heavy rain moved across the area during the early morning hours of June 18th. A car was damaged by the rock slide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains pushed Turtle Creek out of its banks near Austin. The creek crested over a foot above the flood stage at 11.82 feet on the 21st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two lines of severe thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of June 30th. These storms dropped some hail and also produced damaging winds. Quarter sized hail fell near Preston (Fillmore County) and Winona (Winona County). A 69 mph wind gust occurred at the Rochester airport (Olmsted County) and trees and power lines were blown down in Austin (Mower County) and Lanesboro (Fillmore County). A lightning strike started a house fire in Goodview (Winona County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused a house fire in Goodview.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms formed over central North Dakota during the mid evening hours of June 5th. These storms moved into portions of eastern North Dakota along and south of the highway 200 corridor by late evening, then into adjacent portions of west central Minnesota even later. These storms eventually took on a linear structure and produced multiple strong wind reports. There were a few reports of large hail, but they were more isolated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few 4 to 6 inch diameter tree branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June ended with two days of excessive heat across parts of southeast Minnesota. Heat indices topped out between 105 and 115 on both June 29th and 30th, although the high heat indices were more widespread on the 29th. Air temperatures in the upper 80s to middle 90s with dew points in the middle 70s combined to create the excessive heat. The highest calculated heat index was 118 from a mesonet station in Plainview (Wabasha County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices of 105 to 115 were common across Wabasha County on June 29th and 30th. The highest calculated heat index was 118 from a mesonet station in Plainview.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After several heavy rain events in June across the Upper Midwest, floodwaters worked down the Mississippi River. Flooding occurred in late June at Wabasha (Wabasha County) and Winona (Winona County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain events pushed the Mississippi River out of its banks in Wabasha. The river crested over a foot above the flood stage at 13.23 feet on the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms began in western North Dakota early in the evening of June 28th and then swept into northern Minnesota during the early morning hours of the 29th. While there were a few hail reports, this line was primarily a damaging wind producer and featured a measured gust to 80 mph. Numerous trees were damaged by the storms and fell onto homes and power lines.The storms continued to produce strong winds over the open waters of Lake Superior, but weakened to the south as they moved into northwestern Wisconsin.

Two disturbances sliding across the Northern Plains resulted in fairly widespread moderate rainfall from October 9-11. Another episode of minor to moderate flooding ensued with rain totaling from 1.50 to 2.00 inches throughout the basin on saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop and pasture land was inundated as levels on Split Rock Creek climbed to a crest of 2.2 feet above flood stage below Jasper (gage site JSPM5) on October 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two lines of severe thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of June 30th. These storms dropped some hail and also produced damaging winds. Quarter sized hail fell near Preston (Fillmore County) and Winona (Winona County). A 69 mph wind gust occurred at the Rochester airport (Olmsted County) and trees and power lines were blown down in Austin (Mower County) and Lanesboro (Fillmore County). A lightning strike started a house fire in Goodview (Winona County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell west of Preston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening of October 3rd. Some of these storms produced damaging winds that blew down large tree branches near Zumbro Falls (Wabasha County) and trees near Lake City (Wabasha County). Strong winds trailing behind the storms produced a 58 mph wind gust near Zumbro Falls and a 66 mph wind gust on top of St. Mary's Hospital in Rochester (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station northwest of Zumbro Falls measured a 58 mph wind gusts in the strong winds trailing behind a thunderstorm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down onto a power line southwest of Lake City.

Scattered thunderstorms developed with diurnal heating during the afternoon across east central South Dakota and southwest Minnesota beneath a strong elevated mixed layer. With weak deep layer shear, storms did not organize past pulse type and produced small hail and lesser wind gusts at most.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intermittent hail up to dime size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms developed Friday afternoon along a cold front, amidst a moisture rich air mass. Another area of storms developed across the eastern Dakotas, and raced eastward across central, and southern Minnesota. The storms along the front produced a weak landspout tornado near Minnesota Lake. Other storms produced marginal severe hail in the northwest part of the Twin Cities, with larger hail, up to golf ball size, west of Albert Lea.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout tornado moved across an open field and was filmed on video. It did not hit anything.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifting across the area produced scattered thunderstorms across southwest Minnesota. An isolated storm in an environment of 1000 J/kg elevated instability to produce some sub-severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intermittent hail up to penny size fell as a storm glanced Tyler, Minnesota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Colorado Low moved out into the Central Plains in several pieces. As the event began on the afternoon of the 26th, temperatures ranged from 10 to 20 degrees, with east winds of 5 to 15 mph. The first wave of snow spread over the area that afternoon and night, dropping 2 to 6 inches of snow. By the morning of the 27th, a strong thermal gradient set up from northwest to southeast, with Langdon around 10 degrees and Fergus Falls around 30 degrees. Winds had switched to the north, and quickly increased. Snow continued to fall, but mainly south of a line from Valley City to Hillsboro to Fosston to Waskish. Temperatures continued to fall on the 27th, and by 3 am CST on the 28th, they ranged from 4 below zero at Langdon to 12 at Park Rapids. Peak wind speeds on the 27th and 28th ranged from 20 to 40 mph. The most snow fell south of the Highway 2 corridor, where Detroit Lakes had 8.8 inches and Battle Lake and Sebeka had 13.0 inches.

A boundary and weak wind shear were present when scattered showers and thunderstorms developed over portions of south central Minnesota. There were two funnel clouds that were photographed, but neither appeared to have touched down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A skinny funnel extended from a high-based shower or thunderstorm. The photo showed it about about halfway to the ground, but it was not possible to determine whether it ever touched down. Photo relayed by KEYC-TV.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary and weak wind shear were present when scattered showers and thunderstorms developed over portions of south central Minnesota. There were two funnel clouds that were photographed, but neither appeared to have touched down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was photographed near Lake Crystal and relayed by KEYC-TV. The funnel appeared to have been about 1/3 to 1/2 to the ground, but it was not possible to determine whether it ever touched down, and there were no reports of it ever reaching the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary and weak wind shear were present when scattered showers and thunderstorms developed over portions of south central Minnesota. There were two funnel clouds that were photographed, but neither appeared to have touched down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A skinny, rope-like funnel was photographed near Glenville. It appeared to be close to the ground, but it was not possible to determine if it actually reached the ground. Photo relayed by KAAL-TV.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of cold air following a late December winter storm brought wind chills from 20 below to 30 below zero during the evening of December 28 through early morning of December 29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West to northwest winds at times from 5 to 12 mph combined with air temperatures of 10 below to 18 below zero to produce wind chills around -20 to -30 during the evening to early morning hours. Coldest wind chills included Marshall (-28), Windom (-23), Tracy (-23) and Worthington (-22).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure area developed across Kansas and lifted into Iowa by December 2. The leading edge of the system produced some light ice accumulations from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa on early Saturday morning. Snow and strong winds developed during Saturday and Saturday evening, but a downslope impact reduced snow amounts closer to the South Dakota border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to occasionally moderate snowfall produced an accumulation of 2 to 4 inches across Lincoln County, with 3.3 inches at Hendricks. Hazardous travel resulted as winds gusted to 30 to 35 mph and combined with snowfall to produce visibility of a mile or less between 1400-1700CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the area on the 30th, bringing 1 to 5 inches of snow and a strong push of cold air behind it. This resulted in north winds of 30 to 50 mph and ground blizzard conditions by the early morning hours of the 31st, lasting through noon in the northern Red River Valley and through the late afternoon in the southern Red River Valley. With the strong wind and below zero temperatures on the 31st, wind chill readings also dipped to 30 below to 50 below zero.

A very moist and unstable airmass was in place ahead of a shortwave and low-level convergence moving into the area. This lead to storms during the afternoon and evening hours that produced numerous reports of downed trees and power lines due to strong winds along with small hail with the heaviest hit areas stretching from the Brainerd Lakes area to the Iron Range. Power outages from this event lasted for over a day in some areas following the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds blew down a few trees. One of the larger trees was 8 inches in diameter.

North to northwest winds quickly increased on the morning of the 27th, resulting in blizzard or ground blizzard conditions over Minnesota counties along the Red River, stretching from Hallock to Ada to Fergus Falls to Elbow Lake. Since snow continued to fall south of a Valley City to Hillsboro to Fosston to Waskish line, conditions were the worst there, especially after dark. North of that line, ground blizzard conditions were reported in open country. Conditions were better in sheltered areas and within cities and towns. As wind speeds decreased during the early morning hours of the 28th, the blizzard conditions ended. Peak wind speeds ranged from 40 to 60 mph, with the Tenney, Minnesota, Department of Transportation RWIS reporting a 53 mph gust.

The most snow fell south of the Highway 2 corridor, where Detroit Lakes had 8.8 inches and Battle Lake and Sebeka had 13.0 inches.

Arctic air surged southward early on February 4th on gusty northerly winds of 20 to 35 mph.  Dangerous wind chills reached -25 to -35.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northerly winds from 20 to 35 mph brought dangerous wind chills colder than -20 for numerous hours, reaching as low as -34 at Worthington, -32 at Marshall and Slayton, and -29 at Pipestone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening of October 3rd. Some of these storms produced damaging winds that blew down large tree branches near Zumbro Falls (Wabasha County) and trees near Lake City (Wabasha County). Strong winds trailing behind the storms produced a 58 mph wind gust near Zumbro Falls and a 66 mph wind gust on top of St. Mary's Hospital in Rochester (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches were blown down near Zumbro Falls. One of these branches landed on a power line.

A very moist and unstable airmass was in place ahead of a shortwave and low-level convergence moving into the area. This lead to storms during the afternoon and evening hours that produced numerous reports of downed trees and power lines due to strong winds along with small hail with the heaviest hit areas stretching from the Brainerd Lakes area to the Iron Range. Power outages from this event lasted for over a day in some areas following the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees, some blown down by gusty thunderstorm winds. Some of the downed trees  diameters up to 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: North to northwest winds quickly increased on the morning of the 27th, resulting in blizzard or ground blizzard conditions over Minnesota counties along the Red River, stretching from Hallock to Ada to Fergus Falls to Elbow Lake. Since snow continued to fall south of a Valley City to Hillsboro to Fosston to Waskish line, conditions were the worst there, especially after dark. North of that line, ground blizzard conditions were reported in open country. Conditions were better in sheltered areas and within cities and towns. As wind speeds decreased during the early morning hours of the 28th, the blizzard conditions ended. Peak wind speeds ranged from 40 to 60 mph, with the Tenney, Minnesota, Department of Transportation RWIS reporting a 53 mph gust.

An Alberta Clipper and Arctic cold front brought 3 to 6 inches of snow to west central Minnesota from December 30th into the 31st. Strong north winds of 30 to 45 mph came in on the 31st bringing blizzard conditions to the region from late in the morning into the afternoon hours. Travel was significantly disrupted or halted.

December started with an early season snow storm across most of southeast Minnesota. A round of snow during the morning of December 1st was followed by a second round during the evening of the 1st into the early morning of the 2nd. These two rounds deposited between 2 and 10 inches of snow that was accompanied by wind gusts of 35 to 40 mph and drifting of the heavy, wet snow. There was also some occasional freezing rain that occurred with minor ice accumulations. The highest reported snow totals were 10.8 inches in Berne (Dodge County) and 8.5 inches in Hayfield (Dodge County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that between 7 and 10 inches of snow fell across Dodge County. The highest reported total was 10.8 inches in Berne. Wind gusts of 35 to 40 mph created drifting of the heavy, wet snow.

One person was killed in a two vehicle accident east of Austin (Mower County) after a light snow event on December 28th. The two vehicles collided at an intersection that the state police reported to be snow and ice covered. The automated weather observing equipment at the Austin airport reported several hours of light snow during the morning of the 28th, but snowfall amounts from COOP observers were less than a half inch.
