The morning of Saturday, December 1st, a large storm system moved out into the Plains, and into the central part of the country. The surface low moved from Kansas, east-northeast across southern Iowa, northern Illinois, and into southern Michigan by Sunday afternoon. The first band of snow developed prior to noon Saturday along the Iowa, Minnesota border. The area of precipitation expanded north and northwest across southern Minnesota, and west central Wisconsin, before stalling across central Minnesota Saturday night. ||The first band had a mixture of sleet, freezing drizzle and snow as far north as Waseca and Albert Lea. The precipitation quickly changed over to snow, with the heavy snowfall band (1-2 inches per hour), drifting north-northwest across the remainder of southern Minnesota, and portions of west central Wisconsin through 6 pm. This band only lasted 1 to 2 hours, but that was enough to cause numerous car accidents across southern Minnesota. As it moved into the Twin Cities metro area, a quick one to three inches fell before sunset. This band eventually weakened and dissipated in central Minnesota as the main forcing moved northeast. ||During the late afternoon, and through the early evening, lighter snow fell with some sleet, mixed with freezing drizzle in far south central Minnesota. Before midnight, snowfall amounts were already in the 5 to 7 inch range from Mankato to Owatonna, south to the Iowa border. Farther to the north, amounts were in the 2 to 5 inch range. ||After midnight, another concentrated area of light to moderate snow fell across the southern 1/4 of Minnesota. Between 6 AM and 9 AM CST, the area of snow quickly weakened, with mainly flurries before Noon Sunday. ||Locally 8 to 11 inches of snow fell from St. James to Mankato and Faribault, south to the Iowa border. Snowfall amounts quickly fell off to 3 to 5 inches north of this area, with mainly 1 inch or less from west central to central Minnesota. Only areas along the Buffalo Ridge near Canby to far western parts of Redwood and Brown Counties had locally 6 inches. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observers, trained spotters, media and the public reported snowfall amounts across the county ranging from 6 to 9 inches.

Some observations even had unknown precipitation near Long Prairie and Alexandria for a while before the colder air arrived later on the 27th. ||The final wave of precipitation occurred mainly in west central to central Minnesota in the form of snow Thursday night December 27th.||The progression of this storm started in the southern Rockies, and moved out into the plains of Oklahoma, before moving northeast across Kansas, Iowa, southern Minnesota, western Wisconsin, and into the northern Great Lakes in a two day period. Strong east-southeast winds developed on the 26th with very mild air surging northward. This allowed for the precipitation to change over to rain before the colder air arrived late Thursday evening. The pressure gradient strengthened Thursday afternoon and caused a significant increase in wind speeds in west central Minnesota. Gusts of 40 to 50 mph were common in the typical areas of west central Minnesota where surface visibility dropped to a quarter of a mile or less. ||Blizzard conditions were met along the Minnesota, South Dakota border for several hours from late Thursday afternoon through early Friday morning. ||During the height of the storm, there were 9 injuries and 1 fatality due to traffic accidents. The Minnesota State Patrol said it responded to 167 crashes, 259 spin-outs and 13 jackknifed semis on Thursday, December 27.||Some of the heavier snowfall totals from the storm include:|North of Alexandria, 14.5 inches.|Near Long Prairie, 14.2 inches.|Browerville, 13.2 inches. |Sedan, 11.9 inches.|Northwest of Donnelly, 11 inches. |Starbuck, 11 inches. |East of Camp Ripley, 10.5 inches. |Near Little Falls, 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of weather equipment which had visibility less than a quarter of a mile, wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph, and the department of transportation recommending no travel along the Minnesota, South Dakota border, blizzard conditions were met. ||Both the department of transportation sensor along the Minnesota, South Dakota border near Marietta, and the Madison airport had sustained winds of 30 to 40 mph, with occasional gusts of 45 to 50 mph from the late afternoon, through Friday morning around 6 AM CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front slowly moved southward across the Upper Midwest from Sunday evening, December 30th, to the afternoon of Monday, December 31st. Along this front, a few bands of snow developed from west central, through central and northeast Minnesota during the early morning of Monday, December 31st. A few areas received up to 4 to 6 inches during this period, with some freezing precipitation during the onset Sunday evening near Madison, Appleton, and Morris. Most of the snow fell from 9 PM CST Sunday through 6 AM CST Sunday morning. However, areas of blowing snow intensified Sunday morning in west central Minnesota, with gusts of 30 to 45 mph. This created whiteout conditions in some areas, especially in open country. The Minnesota Department of Transportation had several counties in west central Minnesota under a Travel Not Advised status as the storm intensified and caused roads to become hazardous.||Although widespread blowing snow diminished Monday afternoon, skies cleared and temperatures dropped below zero, so wind chill values fell to dangerous levels as wind speeds remained in the 25 to 35 mph range. Slowly toward midnight, winds subsided but temperatures continued to drop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several weather sensors in Lac Qui Parle County showed freezing precipitation develop in the late afternoon of Sunday, December 30th. This area of precipitation changed over to light snow before midnight as the Arctic front moved south of the area. After midnight, wind speed increased to 25 to 35 mph, with occasional gusts of 40 to 45 mph to produced widespread blowing snow. The snow ending after in the morning, but the winds continued, and produced very hazardous driving conditions. The Minnesota Department of Transportation updated their web site with No Travel Advised due to the white out conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a shortwave disturbance rotated around a large southwest U.S. closed low aloft and into confluent flow aloft across the Northern Plains, light wintry precipitation developed on December 31. An arctic front surged southward early in the day with winds increasing to a gusty 30 to 45 mph, creating areas of blowing snow and visibility less than a half mile at times, as well as dangerously cold wind chills by late afternoon and evening of December 31.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated to 1 to 2 inches, and while winds gusted as high as 40 mph at Flandreau and 42 mph at Marshall, periods of blowing snow with visibility of one-half mile or less occurred between 1000-1800CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a shortwave disturbance rotated around a large southwest U.S. closed low aloft and into confluent flow aloft across the Northern Plains, light wintry precipitation developed on December 31. An arctic front surged southward early in the day with winds increasing to a gusty 30 to 45 mph, creating areas of blowing snow and visibility less than a half mile at times, as well as dangerously cold wind chills by late afternoon and evening of December 31.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated to 1 to 2 inches, and while winds gusted as high as 42 mph at Marshall, 40 mph at Flandreau and 38 mph at Tracy, periods of blowing snow with visibility of one-half mile or less occurred between 1000-1800CST.  Travel was not recommended, especially along U.S. Highways 14 and 75 as well as State Highway 19 due to near whiteout conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration winter weather event ensued after Christmas as a full-latitude trough moved through the Rockies on December 26, initiating cyclogenesis as the mid-level wave closed off and lifted northeast from TX into KS by early on December 27. A leading pulse of warm advection ahead of the main system produced a swath of mixed precipitation on the 26th into early on the 27th across southeast SD and southwest MN. This area weakened before the main wave lifted across the area on the 27th and sheared into northern Minnesota by early morning December 28. A significant dry slot pushed across Iowa early on the 27th allowing for a brief change in precipitation to liquid or freezing precipitation. Heavier snowfall redeveloped within the TROWAL during the 27th and continued through the following night across southeast SD into southwest MN. ||As the area of low pressure strengthened moving across Iowa during the afternoon of December 27, winds increased with gusts of 40 to 55 mph common into the early morning of December 28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed early in the morning on December 26, changing briefly to light freezing rain between 1800-2200CST. Snow redeveloped during the overnight hours, becoming moderate to heavy through the afternoon of December 27 with visibility as low as one half mile or less at times. Northwest winds increased with gusts commonly from 35 to 45 mph from 1200CST December 27 through 0900CST December 28. Snowfall accumulated to 10.1 inches at Marshall and 10.0 inches at Minneota, with a peak wind gust of 47 mph at Marshall and 38 mph at Tracy.  Numerous vehicles became stranded and blocked along U.S. Highway 14 during the early morning hours of December 28. No travel was recommended during the height of the storm.  Wind chills reached near -20 from 0600-0900CST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated to around 1 inch, and while winds gusted as high as 42 mph at Jackson, periods of blowing snow with visibility of one-half mile or less occurred between 1200-1800CST.

Travel was not recommended, especially along State Highway 19 due to near whiteout conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level wave and jet entrance region pushed into the Northern Plains, with a tight mid-level frontal boundary stalling across the area for several hours within confluent southwest flow aloft. Unseasonably cold air rushed into the region behind a southward surging low-level frontal boundary, with developing precipitation making a very rapid transition from rain to snow. Snow accumulated quickly in the overnight and early morning hours due to proximity of slightly unstable air aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to occasionally moderate snow accumulated to 2 to 6 inches across most of southwest Minnesota, with the greatest amounts near Interstate 90.  Visibility dropped to a mile or less for almost 2 hours at Luverne, creating hazardous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon along a cold front across eastern Minnesota. These storms developed west of the Twin Cities metro area, and quickly moved eastward. Some damage occurred on the south and west side of the metro area. The damage was mainly to downed trees. This line also produced a severe wind gust at the Minneapolis, St. Paul International airport of 61 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down near the Eden Prairie, Chanhassen line. The tree landed on a car. There was a measured wind report of 45 mph at the Flying Cloud airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered tree damage occurred southwest of downtown St. Paul, near Harriet Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An autumn storm hit the Northland and brought strong winds, driving rain and some snow. The hardest hit area was the western end of Lake Superior, where winds gusting over 60 mph caused tremendous waves to crash on to shore. The crashing waves and storm surge, aided by an elevated water level of Lake Superior, caused considerable damage to Duluth���s Lakewalk and flooded Duluth���s Canal Park. Traffic was restricted when South Lake Avenue was flooded at the entrance to the aerial lift bridge. There was also major damage to Duluth���s Brighton Beach with damage to the road and major erosion to the shoreline as well as a tremendous amount of debris that was strewn across the park from the powerful waves. The high winds, heavy rain and strong storm surge also caused some county roads to flood along the north shore of Lake Superior and in downtown Grand Marais. Numerous trees and branches were blown down across the Northland, with the hardest hit areas along the north and south shore where winds were the strongest. Many areas lost power. The winds increased during the evening of October 9th with northeast wind gusts reaching over 30 mph. By morning winds gusts were near 50 mph at the Duluth Airport. The winds remained gusty in the 35 to 50 mph range all day and decreased to below 30 mph after 7:00 pm. Some highest wind gusts include 86 mph from the Canadian freighter Algowood off the north shore near Castle Danger, 64 mph and 20 foot waves from the Canadian ship the CSL Assinibione as it was anchored near Duluth, 55 mph in east Duluth, 52 mph at Glensheen Mansion and 46 mph at the Duluth Airport.  All of these highest gusts were recorded the morning of October 10th.  At 10 am on the 10th, the Areal Lift Bridge in Duluth���s Canal Park recorded a steady wind from 40 to 45 mph and 5 to 9 feet waves. Later that afternoon waves were 10 feet in the ship canal. The storm was caused by a strong low pressure area that developed in the Texas Panhandle on October 9th. By Wednesday, October 10th, the center of the low was over far southern Minnesota while high pressure as situated across much of Canada. Temperatures to the north of the low were in the 40s and south of the low they were in the 70s. This strong gradient in temperature and pressure helped create the strong northeast winds that blew across Lake Superior. The long fetch and the high Lake Superior water level contributed to the high waves and storm surge. Lake Superior levels were almost a foot higher than October normal. By Thursday, October 11th, the low���s center was over eastern Lake Superior which caused the winds to become northwesterly and calmed the Lake Superior waves.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Onshore waves and strong winds off of Lake Superior pummeled the shoreline around Duluth throwing rocks and upending concrete-based walkways. Portions of the the Duluth Lakewalk, Brighton Beach, Canal Park, and Park Point were damaged, many of which were damaged in last year's storm and had not yet been repaired. Flooding was seen in Canal Park and was also observed 8 miles upstream on the St. Louis River due to the winds and water pushing upstream. Damages in the city of Duluth alone to public infrastructure was estimated at $18.4 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the South Dakota/Wyoming border during the morning hours moved quickly northeast through the day to just north of Lake Superior by the evening. This low pulled a cold front through the Minnesota Arrowhead region and northwest Wisconsin. A few strong to severe storms developed ahead of the front, but no damage reports were received. Strong winds accompanied the cold front as it moved through the region. Wind gusts to 45-55 mph were reported with the cold front passage with the strongest winds being seen between 9PM and 1AM. Numerous trees were downed leading to power outages and damage in Cook County with substantial damage being reported at a campground near Grand Marais.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds associated with a cold frontal passage lead to numerous downed trees that knocked out power to over 1,000 customers in the area. Some customers did not have power restored for over 2 days, making it the longest outage in the area in almost a decade. An RV park near the Bay of Grand Marias sustained damage with trees falling onto RVs. A peak gust of 56 mph was reported at 10:13 pm at the Grand Marais Harbor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two disturbances sliding across the Northern Plains resulted in fairly widespread moderate rainfall from October 9-11. Another episode of minor to moderate flooding ensued with rain totaling from 1.50 to 2.00 inches throughout the basin on saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop and pasture land was inundated as levels on Split Rock Creek climbed to a crest of 2.2 feet above flood stage below Jasper (gage site JSPM5) on October 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level frontogenesis within the entrance region of a jet streak across northern Wisconsin brought rapid onset to precipitation during the afternoon and evening of November 16. Gradual loss of deep saturation resulted in a trailing area of light freezing rain or freezing drizzle behind the main light snow band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of late evening freezing drizzle resulted in a light glaze on roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level frontogenesis within the entrance region of a jet streak across northern Wisconsin brought rapid onset to precipitation during the afternoon and evening of November 16. Gradual loss of deep saturation resulted in a trailing area of light freezing rain or freezing drizzle behind the main light snow band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Occasionally moderate snowfall quickly totaled 2.0 inches at Worthington during the evening hours. Precipitation ended as a brief period of freezing drizzle late evening. As temperatures fell rapidly, flash freezing of initially melted precipitation resulted in treacherous roadways, causing several minor accidents. A driver lost control on icy roads and entered the median around 0245CST November 17, resulting in a minor injury (indirect) to a 30-year-old female passenger. A single-car rollover accident north of Bigelow around 0530CST November 17 resulted in minor injuries (indirect) to a 27-year-old female driver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from northern Missouri on November 4th northeast to the Bayfield Peninsula by the morning of the 5th. This low positioning, along with Lake Superior surface temperatures in the 40s, set up favorable conditions for lake effect snow along the North and South Shores of Lake Superior. While most locations saw less than 4, one location along the terrain ridge adjacent to Lake Superior's North Shore recorded 6 of accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowfall total of 6 was recorded 3 miles east of Finland, Minnesota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The lake effect snow of November 9-10 was the first major event along western Lake Superior for the season. A band of lake effect snow developed over the North Shore of Lake Superior along the Gunflint Trail to Grand Portage. Grand Portage reported 12 at 0845 on the 9th, while the Golden Eagle Lodge on the Gunflint Trail reported 7.4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 12 was reported 1 mile NNW of Grand Portage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level frontogenesis within the entrance region of a jet streak across northern Wisconsin brought rapid onset to precipitation during the afternoon and evening of November 16. Gradual loss of deep saturation resulted in a trailing area of light freezing rain or freezing drizzle behind the main light snow band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall quickly totaled 3.0 inches at Marshall and Tracy, and 2.0 inches at Minneota during the late afternoon and evening hours.  Precipitation ended as a brief period of freezing drizzle. As temperatures fell rapidly toward sunset, flash freezing of initially melted precipitation resulted in treacherous roadways, causing several minor accidents. A two-car accident west of Balaton during the midday hours of November 18 resulted in minor injuries (indirect) to a 79-year-old female passenger.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level frontogenesis within the entrance region of a jet streak across northern Wisconsin brought rapid onset to precipitation during the afternoon and evening of November 16. Gradual loss of deep saturation resulted in a trailing area of light freezing rain or freezing drizzle behind the main light snow band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate snowfall quickly totaled 2.5 inches southeast of Astoria and near Canby, as well as 2.0 inches at Minneota during the late afternoon and early evening. Precipitation ended as a brief period of freezing drizzle which resulted in a light glaze of ice on cleared surfaces by late evening. As temperatures dropped rapidly after snow began, initially melted precipitation due to mild surface conditions experienced flash freezing and produced treacherous roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level frontogenesis within the entrance region of a jet streak across northern Wisconsin brought rapid onset to precipitation during the afternoon and evening of November 16. EVENT_NARRATIVE: While only an inch of snow fell at Pipestone and near Edgerton during light to moderate snowfall in the late afternoon and early evening, precipitation changed to a light freezing drizzle by mid-evening.  As temperatures fell rapidly, a glaze of ice resulted in treacherous road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level frontogenesis within the entrance region of a jet streak across northern Wisconsin brought rapid onset to precipitation during the afternoon and evening of November 16. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate snowfall totaled quickly to 3.3 inches 2 miles northeast of Lakefield during the evening hours.  As temperatures fell quickly during the evening, snowfall which had melted experienced flash freezing and produced treacherous road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level frontogenesis within the entrance region of a jet streak across northern Wisconsin brought rapid onset to precipitation during the afternoon and evening of November 16. Gradual loss of deep saturation resulted in a trailing area of light freezing rain or freezing drizzle behind the main light snow band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate snowfall quickly totaled to 3.2 inches at Windom during the late afternoon and evening.  Roads quickly became treacherous as temperatures fell rapidly by evening, causing flash freezing of earlier melted snowfall.  Several minor accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-level frontogenesis within the entrance region of a jet streak across northern Wisconsin brought rapid onset to precipitation during the afternoon and evening of November 16. Gradual loss of deep saturation resulted in a trailing area of light freezing rain or freezing drizzle behind the main light snow band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate snowfall quickly totaled 2.0 inches at Lake Wilson during the late afternoon and early evening, with precipitation mixing with light freezing drizzle before ending.  A glaze of icing occurred as temperatures fell rapidly during the evening, resulting in treacherous roads and several minor accidents.
